13.note: tc The LXX, Syriac, Vulgate, and certain mss of Smr have “your” (plural) rather than “their” (cf. v. 10 above).

13.anchor: midst who hunts a wild animal
13.note: tn Heb “[wild] game of animal.”

13.anchor: or a bird that may be eaten
13.note: tn That is, it must be a clean animal, not an unclean animal (cf. Lev 11).

14.anchor: for the life of all flesh is its blood.
14.note: tn Heb “for the life/soul (נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh) of all flesh, its blood in its life/soul (נֶפֶשׁ) it is.” The LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate leave out “in its life/soul,” which would naturally yield “for the life of all flesh, its blood it is” (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 261, 263). The present translation is something of an oversimplification, but the meaning is basically the same in any case. Cf. NRSV “For the life of every creature – its blood is its life.”

14.anchor: So I have said to the Israelites: You must not eat the blood of any living thing
14.note: tn Heb “of all flesh” (also later in this verse). See the note on “every living thing” in v. 11.

14.anchor: because the life of every living thing is its blood – all who eat it will be cut off.
14.note: tn For remarks on the “cut off” penalty see the note on v. 4 above.

15.anchor: “‘Any person
15.note: tn Heb “And any soul” (נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh).

15.anchor: who eats an animal that has died of natural causes
15.note: tn Heb “carcass,” referring to the carcass of an animal that has died on its own, not the carcass of an animal slaughtered for sacrifice or killed by wild beasts. This has been clarified in the translation by supplying the phrase “of natural causes”; cf. NAB “that died of itself”; TEV “that has died a natural death.”

15.anchor: or an animal torn by beasts, whether a native citizen or a foreigner,
15.note: tn Heb “in the native or in the sojourner.”

16.anchor: But if he does not wash his clothes
16.note: tn The words “his clothes” are not in the Hebrew text, but are repeated in the translation for clarity.

16.anchor: and does not bathe his body, he will bear his punishment for iniquity.’”
16.note: tn Heb “and he shall bear his iniquity.” The rendering “bear the punishment for the iniquity” reflects the use of the word “iniquity” to refer to the punishment for iniquity. This is sometimes referred to as the consequential use of the term (cf. Lev 5:17; 7:18; 10:17; etc.).sn For the interpretation of this verse reflected in the present translation, see the remarks on Lev 5:1 in J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:292-97.

3.anchor: You must not do as they do in the land of Egypt where you have been living,
3.note: tn Heb “As the work [or “deed”] of the land of Egypt, which you were dwelling in it, you must not do.”

3.anchor: and you must not do as they do in the land of Canaan into which I am about to bring you;
3.note: tn Heb “and as the work [or “deed”] of the land of Canaan which I am bringing you to there, you must not do.” The participle “I am bringing” is inceptive; the Lord is “about to” bring them into the land of Canaan, as opposed to their having dwelt previously in the land of Egypt (see the first part of the verse).

3.anchor: you must not
3.note: tn Heb “and you shall not walk.”

4.anchor: You must observe my regulations
4.note: tn Heb “My regulations you shall do”; KJV, NASB “my judgments”; NRSV “My ordinances”; NIV, TEV “my laws.”sn The Hebrew term translated “regulation” (מִשְׁפָּט, mishpat) refers to the set of regulations about to be set forth in the following chapters (cf. Lev 19:37; 20:22; 25:18; 26:46). Note especially the thematic and formulaic relationships between the introduction here in Lev 18:1-5 and the paraenesis in Lev 20:22-26, both of which refer explicitly to the corrupt nations and the need to separate from them by keeping the Lord’s regulations.

4.anchor: and you must be sure to walk in my statutes.
4.note: tn Heb “and my statutes you shall keep [or “watch; guard”] to walk in them.”

5.anchor: So you must keep
5.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep.”

5.anchor: my statutes and my regulations; anyone who does so will live by keeping them.
5.note: tn Heb “which the man shall do them and shall live in them.” The term for “a man, human being; mankind” (אָדָם, ’adam; see the note on Lev 1:2) in this case refers to any person among “mankind,” male or female. The expression וָחַי (vakhay, “and shall live”) looks like the adjective “living” so it is written וְחָיָה (vÿkhayah) in Smr, but the MT form is simply the same verb written as a double ayin verb (see HALOT 309 s.v. חיה qal and GKC 218 §76.i; cf. Lev 25:35).

6.anchor: “‘No man is to approach any close relative
6.note: tn Heb “Man, man shall not draw near to any flesh (שְׁאֵר, shÿ’er) of his body/flesh (בָּשָׂר, basar).” The repetition of the word “man” is distributive, meaning “any (or “every”) man” (GKC 395-96 §123.c; cf. Lev 15:2). The two words for “flesh” are combined to refer to emphasize the physical familial relatedness (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 282, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 119).

6.anchor: to have sexual intercourse with her.
6.note: tn Heb “to uncover [her] nakedness” (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV), which is clearly euphemistic for sexual intercourse (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 282, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 119). This expression occurs a number of times in the following context and is generally translated “have sexual intercourse with [someone],” although in the case of the father mentioned in the following verse the expression may be connected to the shame or disgrace that would belong to the father whose wife’s sexuality is violated by his son. See the note on the word “mother” in v. 7.

sn The general statement prohibiting sexual intercourse between close relatives serves as an opening summary statement for the following section, which gives details concerning which degrees of relationship are specifically forbidden.

7.anchor: You must not
7.note: tn The verbal negative here is the same as that used in the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:4-5, 7, 13-17). It suggests permanent prohibition rather than a simple negative command and could, therefore, be rendered “must not” here and throughout the following section as it is in vv. 3-4 above.

7.anchor: expose your father’s nakedness by having sexual intercourse with your mother.
7.note: tn Heb “The nakedness of your father and [i.e., even] the nakedness of your mother you shall not uncover.” sn Commentators suggest that the point of referring to the father’s nakedness is that the mother’s sexuality belongs to the father and is forbidden to the son on that account (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 120, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 294). The expression may, however, derive from the shame of nakedness when exposed. If one exposes his mother’s nakedness to himself it is like openly exposing the father’s nakedness (cf. Gen 9:22-23 with the background of Gen 2:25 and 3:7, 21). The same essential construction is used in v. 10 where the latter explanation makes more sense than the former.

8.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with your father’s wife; she is your father’s nakedness.
8.note: tn Heb “the nakedness of your father she is.” See the note on v. 7 above. This law refers to another wife of the man’s father, who is not that man’s mother. The laws in the Pentateuch sometimes assume the possibility that a man may have more than one wife (cf., e.g., Deut 21:15-17).

9.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with your sister, whether she is your father’s daughter or your mother’s daughter,
9.note: tn Heb “the daughter of your father or the daughter of your mother.”

9.anchor: whether she is born in the same household or born outside it;
9.note: tn Heb “born of house or born of outside.” CEV interprets as “whether you grew up together or not” (cf. also TEV, NLT).

9.anchor: you must not have sexual intercourse with either of them.
9.note: tc Several medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, LXX, and Syriac have “her nakedness” rather than “their nakedness,” thus agreeing with singular “sister” at the beginning of the verse.tn For a smooth English translation “either of” was added.

10.anchor: You must not expose the nakedness of your son’s daughter or your daughter’s daughter by having sexual intercourse with them, because they are your own nakedness.
10.note: sn That is, to have sexual intercourse with one’s granddaughter would be like openly exposing one’s own shameful nakedness (see the note on v. 7 above).

11.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with the daughter of your father’s wife born of your father; she is your sister. You must not have intercourse with her.
11.note: tn Heb “The nakedness of the daughter of your father’s wife born of your father, she is your sister; you must not uncover her nakedness.” That is, a half sister, the daughter of the man’s father by another wife, who is not the man’s mother, is to be considered a true sister. Therefore, the man must not have sexual intercourse with her.

12.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with your father’s sister; she is your father’s flesh.
12.note: tc A few medieval Hebrew mss, the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate all read “because she is the flesh of your father,” like the MT of v. 13.

14.anchor: You must not expose the nakedness of your father’s brother; you must not approach his wife to have sexual intercourse with her.
14.note: tn Heb “you must not draw near to his wife.” In the context this refers to approaching one’s aunt to have sexual intercourse with her, so this has been specified in the translation for clarity.

14.anchor: She is your aunt.
14.note: tn As in v. 12 (see the note there), some mss and versions have “because she is your aunt.”

16.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with your brother’s wife; she is your brother’s nakedness.
16.note: sn Regarding the last clause, see the notes on vv. 7 and 10 above.

17.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with both a woman and her daughter; you must not take as wife either her son’s daughter or her daughter’s daughter to have intercourse with them.
17.note: tn Heb “You must not uncover the nakedness of both a woman and her daughter; the daughter of her son and the daughter of her daughter you must not take to uncover her nakedness.” Translating “her” as “them” provides consistency in the English. In this kind of context, “take” means to “take in marriage” (cf. also v. 18). The LXX and Syriac have “their nakedness,” referring to the nakedness of the woman’s granddaughters, rather than the nakedness of the woman herself.

17.anchor: They are closely related to her
17.note: tc Heb “they are her flesh.” The LXX reads “your” here (followed by NRSV). If the LXX reading were followed by the present translation, the result would be “They are closely related to you.”

17.anchor: – it is lewdness.
17.note: tn The term rendered “lewdness” almost always carries a connotation of cunning, evil device, and divisiveness (cf. HALOT 272 s.v. I זִמָּה 2, “infamy”), and is closely associated with sexual and religious infidelity (cf., e.g., Lev 19:29; 20:14; Job 31:11; Jer 13:27; Ezek 16:27; 22:9). Recent English versions differ on how they handle this: NAB “would be shameful”; CEV “would make you unclean”; NIV “wickedness”; NLT “horrible wickedness”; NRSV “depravity”; TEV “incest.”

18.anchor: You must not take a woman in marriage and then marry her sister as a rival wife
18.note: tn Or “as a concubine”; Heb “And a woman to her sister you shall not take to be a second wife [or “to be a concubine”].” According to HALOT 1059 s.v. III צרר, the infinitive “to be a second wife” (לִצְרֹר, litsror) is a denominative verb from II צָרָה A (“concubine; second wife”), which, in turn, derives from II צר “to treat with hostility” (cf. J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 283, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 122).

18.anchor: while she is still alive,
18.note: tn Heb “on her in her life.”

19.anchor: “‘You must not approach a woman in her menstrual impurity
19.note: tn Heb “in the menstruation of her impurity”; NIV “during the uncleanness of her monthly period.”

20.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse
20.note: tn Heb “And to the wife of your fellow citizen you shall not give your layer for seed.” The meaning of “your layer” (שְׁכָבְתְּךָ, shÿkhavtÿkha) is uncertain (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 122, “you shall not place your layer of semen”; but cf. also J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 283, and the literature cited there for the rendering, “you shall not give your penis for seed”).

21.anchor: You must not give any of your children as an offering to Molech,
21.note: tn Heb “And from your seed you shall not give to cause to pass over to Molech.” Smr (cf. also the LXX) has “to cause to serve” rather than “to cause to pass over.” For detailed remarks on Molech and Molech worship see N. H. Snaith, Leviticus and Numbers (NCBC), 87-88; P. J. Budd, Leviticus (NCBC), 259-60; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 333-37, and the literature cited there. It could refer to either human sacrifice or a devotion of children to some sort of service of Molech, perhaps of a sexual sort (cf. Lev 20:2-5; 2 Kgs 23:10, etc.). The inclusion of this prohibition against Molech worship here may be due to some sexual connection of this kind, or perhaps simply to the lexical link between זֶרַע (zera’) meaning “seed, semen” in v. 20 but “offspring” in v. 21.

21.anchor: so that you do not profane
21.note: tn Heb “and you shall not profane.” Regarding “profane,” see the note on Lev 10:10 above.

22.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse with a male as one has sexual intercourse with a woman;
22.note: tn Heb “And with a male you shall not lay [as the] lyings of a woman” (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 123). The specific reference here is to homosexual intercourse between males.

22.anchor: it is a detestable act.
22.note: tn The Hebrew term תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, rendered “detestable act”) refers to the repugnant practices of foreigners, whether from the viewpoint of other peoples toward the Hebrews (e.g., Gen 43:32; 46:34; Exod 8:26) or of the Lord toward other peoples (see esp. Lev 18:26-27, 29-30). It can also designate, as here, detestable acts that might be perpetrated by the native peoples (it is used again in reference to homosexuality in Lev 20:13; cf. also its use for unclean food, Deut 14:3; idol worship, Isa 41:24; remarriage to a former wife who has been married to someone else in between, Deut 24:4).

23.anchor: You must not have sexual intercourse
23.note: tn See the note on v. 20 above.

23.anchor: with any animal to become defiled with it, and a woman must not stand before an animal to have sexual intercourse with it;
23.note: tn Heb “to copulate with it” (cf. Lev 20:16).

23.anchor: it is a perversion.
23.note: tn The Hebrew term תֶּבֶל (tevel, “perversion”) derives from the verb “to mix; to confuse” and therefore refers to illegitimate mixtures of species or violation of the natural order of things.

24.anchor: “‘Do not defile yourselves with any of these things, for the nations which I am about to drive out before you
24.note: tn Heb “which I am sending away (Piel participle of שָׁלַח [shalakh, “to send”]) from your faces.” The rendering here takes the participle as anticipatory of the coming conquest events.

25.anchor: Therefore
25.note: tn Heb “And.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative or even inferential force here.

25.anchor: the land has become unclean and I have brought the punishment for its iniquity upon it,
25.note: tn Heb “and I have visited its [punishment for] iniquity on it.” See the note on Lev 17:16 above.

26.anchor: You yourselves must obey
26.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep, you.” The latter emphatic personal pronoun “you” is left out of a few medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate.

26.anchor: my statutes and my regulations and must not do any of these abominations, both the native citizen and the resident foreigner in your midst,
26.note: tn Heb “the native and the sojourner”; NIV “The native-born and the aliens”; NAB “whether natives or resident aliens.”

27.anchor: for the people who were in the land before you have done all these abominations,
27.note: tn Heb “for all these abominations the men of the land who were before you have done.”

28.anchor: So do not make the land vomit you out because you defile it
28.note: tn Heb “And the land will not vomit you out in your defiling it.”

28.anchor: just as it has vomited out the nations
28.note: tc The MT reads the singular “nation” and is followed by ASV, NASB, NRSV; the LXX, Syriac, and Targum have the plural “nations” (cf. v. 24).

29.anchor: For if anyone does any of these abominations, the persons who do them will be cut off from the midst of their people.
29.note: sn Regarding the “cut off” penalty see the note on Lev 7:20.

30.anchor: You must obey my charge to not practice any of the abominable statutes
30.note: tn Heb “to not do from the statutes of the detestable acts.”

30.anchor: that have been done before you, so that you do not
30.note: tn Heb “and you will not.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

1.anchor: Lord
1.note: tn Heb “in their drawing near to the faces of the Lord.” The rendering here relies on the use of this expression for the very “presence” of God in Exod 33:14-15 and in the Lev 9:24-10:2 passage, where the Nadab and Abihu catastrophe referred to here is narrated.

2.anchor: said to Moses: “Tell Aaron your brother that he must not enter at any time into the holy place inside the veil-canopy
2.note: tn Heb “into the holy place from house to the veil-canopy.” In this instance, the Hebrew term “the holy place” refers to “the most holy place” (lit. “holy of holies”), since it is the area “inside the veil-canopy” (cf. Exod 26:33-34). The Hebrew term פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet) is usually translated “veil” or “curtain,” but it seems to have stretched not only in front of but also over the top of the ark of the covenant which stood behind and under it inside the most holy place, and thus formed more of a canopy than simply a curtain (see R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 3:687-89).

2.anchor: in front of the atonement plate
2.note: tn Heb “to the faces of the atonement plate.” The exact meaning of the Hebrew term כַּפֹּרֶת (kapporet) here rendered “atonement plate” is much debated. The traditional “mercy seat” (KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV) does not suit the cognate relationship between this term and the Piel verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, “to make atonement, to make expiation”). The translation of the word should also reflect the fact that the most important atonement procedures on the Day of Atonement were performed in relation to it. Since the Lord would “appear in the cloud over the atonement plate,” and since it was so closely associated with the ark of the covenant (the ark being his “footstool”; cf. 1 Chr 28:2 and Ps 132:7-8), one could take it to be the place of his throne at which he accepts atonement. See J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:1014; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 234-35; and R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:691, 699. Cf. NIV “the atonement cover”; NCV “the lid on the Ark”; NLT “the Ark’s cover – the place of atonement.”

3.anchor: “In this way Aaron is to enter into the sanctuary – with a young bull
3.note: tn Heb “with a bull, a son of the herd.”

3.anchor: for a sin offering
3.note: sn See the note on Lev 4:3 regarding the term “sin offering.”

3.anchor: and a ram for a burnt offering.
3.note: sn For the “burnt offering” see the note on Lev 1:3.

4.anchor: He must put on a holy linen tunic,
4.note: sn The term “tunic” refers to a shirt-like garment worn next to the skin and, therefore, put on first (cf. Exod 28:4, 39-40; 29:5, 8; 39:27). It covered the upper body only. For detailed remarks on the terminology for the priestly clothing in this verse (except the “linen leggings”) see the notes on Lev 8:7-9 and the literature cited there.

4.anchor: linen leggings are to cover his body,
4.note: tn Heb “shall be on his flesh.” As in many instances in Lev 15, the term “flesh” or “body” here is euphemistic for the male genitals (J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1017, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 222; cf. the note on Lev 15:2), which the priest must be careful not to expose during such ritual procedures (see Exod 20:26 with 28:42-43).

4.anchor: and he is to wrap himself with a linen sash
4.note: sn The sash fastened the tunic around the waist (Exod 28:4, 39; 29:9; 39:29).

4.anchor: and wrap his head with a linen turban.
4.note: tn Heb “and in a turban of linen he shall wrap.” sn The turban consisted of wound up linen (cf. Exod 28:4, 37, 39; 29:6; 39:31; Lev 16:4). It is usually thought to be a “turban,” but it might be only a “turban-like headband” wound around the forehead area (HALOT 624 s.v. מִצְנֶפֶת).

4.anchor: They are holy garments, so he must bathe
4.note: tn Heb “and he shall bathe….”

5.anchor: He must also take
5.note: tn Heb “And he shall take.”

5.anchor: two male goats
5.note: tn Heb “he-goats of goats”; CEV “two goats, both of them males.”

7.anchor: He must then take the two goats
7.note: tn Heb “the two he-goats,” referred to as “two he-goats of goats” in v. 5.

8.anchor: and Aaron is to cast lots over the two goats,
8.note: tn Heb “and Aaron shall give lots on the two he-goats.” See the note on Lev 8:8 for the priestly casting of lots in Israel and the explanation in B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 102, on Lev 16:8-9. J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:1019-20, suggests, however, that the expression here signifies that, the lots having been cast, the priest was to literally “place” (Heb “give”) the one marked “for the Lord” on the head of the goat to be sacrificed and the one marked “for Azazel” on the head of the one to be released in the wilderness in order to avoid confusing them later in the ritual sequence.

8.anchor: and one lot for Azazel.
8.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term עֲזָאזֵל (’aza’zel, four times in the OT, all of them in this chapter; vv. 8, 10 [2 times], and 26) is much debated. There are three or perhaps four major views (see the summaries and literature cited in J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1020-21; B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 102; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 237-38; D. P. Wright, The Disposal of Impurity [SBLDS], 21-25; M. V. Van Pelt and W. C. Kaiser, NIDOTTE 3:362-63; and M. S. Moore, NIDOTTE 4:421-22). (1) Some derive the term from a combination of the Hebrew word עֵז (’ez, “goat”; i.e., the word for “goats” in v. 5) and אָזַל (’azal, “to go away”), meaning “the goat that departs” or “scapegoat” (cf., e.g., the LXX and KJV, NASB, NIV, NLT). This meaning suits the ritual practice of sending the so-called “scapegoat” away into the wilderness (vv. 10, 21-22, 26). Similarly, some derive the term from Arabic ’azala (“to banish, remove”), meaning “entire removal” as an abstract concept (see BDB 736 s.v. עֲזָאזֵל). (2) Some see the term as a description of the wilderness area to which the goat was dispatched, deriving it somehow from Arabic ’azazu (“rough ground”) or perhaps עָזָז, (’azaz, “to be strong, fierce”). (3) The most common view among scholars today is that it is the proper name of a particular demon (perhaps even the Devil himself) associated with the wilderness desert regions. Levine has proposed that it may perhaps derive from a reduplication of the ז (zayin) in עֵז combined with אֵל (’el, “mighty”), meaning “mighty goat.” The final consonantal form of עֲזָאזֵל would have resulted from the inversion of the א (aleph) with the second ז. He makes the point that the close association between עֵז and שְׂעִירִים (shÿ’irim), which seems to refer to “goat-demons” of the desert in Lev 17:7 (cf. Isa 13:21, etc.), should not be ignored in the derivation of Azazel, although the term ultimately became the name of “the demonic ruler of the wilderness.” The latter view is supported by the parallel between the one goat “for (לְ, lamed preposition) the Lord” and the one “for (לְ) Azazel” here in v. 8. The rendering as a proper name has been tentatively accepted here (cf. ASV, NAB, NRSV, TEV, CEV). Perhaps a play on words between the proper name and the term for “goat” has occurred so that the etymology has become obscure. Even if a demon or the demonic realm is the source for the name, however, there is no intention here of appeasing the demons. The goal is to remove the impurity and iniquity from the community in order to avoid offending the Lord and the repercussions of such (see esp. vv. 21-22 and cf. Lev 15:31).

9.note: tn Heb “which the lot has gone up on it for the Lord.”

10.anchor: but the goat which has been designated by lot for Azazel is to be stood alive
10.note: tn The LXX has “he shall stand it” (cf. v. 7).

10.anchor: to make atonement on it by sending it away to Azazel into the wilderness.
10.note: tn Heb “to make atonement on it to send it away to Azazel toward the wilderness.”

12.anchor: Lord
12.note: tn Heb “and he shall take the fullness of the censer, coals of fire, from on the altar from to the faces of the Lord.”

12.anchor: and a full double handful of finely ground fragrant incense,
12.note: tn Heb “and the fullness of the hollow of his two hands, finely ground fragrant incense.”

12.anchor: and bring them inside the veil-canopy.
12.note: tn Heb “and he shall bring from house to the veil-canopy.”

13.anchor: , and the cloud of incense will cover the atonement plate which is above the ark of the testimony,
13.note: tn The text here has only “above the testimony,” but this is surely a shortened form of “above the ark of the testimony” (see Exod 25:22 etc.; cf. Lev 16:2). The term “testimony” in this expression refers to the ark as the container of the two stone tablets with the Ten Commandments written on them (see Exod 25:16 with Deut 10:1, 5, etc.).

13.anchor: so that he will not die.
13.note: tn Heb “and he will not die,” but it is clear that the purpose for the incense cloud was to protect the priest from death in the presence of the Lord (cf. vv. 1-2 above).

14.anchor: Then he is to take some of the blood of the bull and sprinkle it with his finger on the eastern face of the atonement plate,
14.note: tn Heb “on the faces of the atonement plate toward the east.” Some have taken this to mean that the ark was stationed just behind the veil-canopy on the eastern side of the most holy place. Thus, the high priest would need to enter and walk toward the west end of the most holy place and then turn eastward in order to face the ark and sprinkle the blood in an eastward direction. The rendering here, however, requires that the ark was stationed on the western end, or perhaps in the middle of the area, so that as the priest entered he was already facing the ark and would sprinkle the blood on the eastern face of the atonement plate, in a westward direction (see, e.g., J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 239 versus J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1032).

14.anchor: and in front of the atonement plate he is to sprinkle some of the blood seven times with his finger.
14.note: sn Presumably in this case the blood was sprinkled seven times on the ground in front of the ark on which the atonement plate was mounted.

15.anchor: “He must then slaughter the sin offering goat which is for the people. He is to bring its blood inside the veil-canopy,
15.note: tn Heb “and he shall bring its blood into from house to the veil-canopy.”

16.anchor: So
16.note: tn Heb “And.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative or even inferential force here.

16.anchor: he is to make atonement for the holy place from the impurities of the Israelites and from their transgressions with regard to all their sins,
16.note: tn Heb “to all their sins.”

17.anchor: Nobody is to be in the Meeting Tent
17.note: tn Heb “And all man shall not be in the tent of meeting.” The term for “a man, human being” (אָדָם, ’adam; see the note on Lev 1:2) refers to any person among “mankind,” male or female.

18.anchor: “Then
18.note: tn Heb “And.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) indicates the sequence of events here.

18.anchor: and make atonement for it. He is to take
18.note: tn Heb “And he shall take.”

19.anchor: Then he is to sprinkle on it some of the blood with his finger seven times, and cleanse and consecrate it
19.note: tn Heb “and he shall purify it and he shall consecrate it.”

20.anchor: “When he has finished purifying the holy place,
20.note: tn Heb “And he shall finish from atoning the holy place.” In this case, the “holy place” etc. are direct objects of the verb “to atone” (cf. v. 33a below). In this case, therefore, the basic meaning of the verb (i.e., “to purge” or “wipe clean”) comes to the forefront. When the prepositions עַל (’al) or בֲּעַד (ba’ad) occur with the verb כִּפֶּר (kipper) the purging is almost always being done “for” or “on behalf of” priests or people (see the note on Lev 1:4 as well as R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:698, the literature cited there, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 110, for more details).

21.anchor: Aaron is to lay his two hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the Israelites and all their transgressions in regard to all their sins,
21.note: tn Heb “transgressions to all their sins.”

21.anchor: and thus he is to put them
21.note: tn Heb “and he shall give them.”

21.anchor: on the head of the goat and send it away into the wilderness by the hand of a man standing ready.
21.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term עִתִּי (’itti) is uncertain. It is apparently related to עֵת (’et, “time”), and could perhaps mean either that he has been properly “appointed” (i.e., designated) for the task (e.g., NIV and NRSV) or “ready” (e.g., NASB and NEB).

22.anchor: The goat is to bear on itself all their iniquities into an inaccessible land,
22.note: tn The Hebrew term rendered “inaccessible” derives from a root meaning “to cut off” (cf. NAB “an isolated region”). Another possible translation would be “infertile land” (see HALOT 187 s.v. *גָּזֵּר and cf. NRSV “a barren region”; NLT “a desolate land.”

22.anchor: so he is to send the goat away
22.note: tn Heb “and he [the man (standing) ready, v. 21] shall send the goat away.”

23.anchor: “Aaron must then enter
23.note: tn Heb “And Aaron shall enter.”

24.anchor: Then he must bathe his body in water in a holy place, put on his clothes, and go out and make his burnt offering and the people’s burnt offering. So he is to make atonement
24.note: tn Heb “And he shall make atonement.”

24.anchor: on behalf of himself and the people.
24.note: tn Heb “on behalf of himself and on behalf of the people.” After “on behalf of himself” the LXX adds the expected “and on behalf of his household” (cf. vv. 6, 11, and 17).

25.anchor: “Then he is to offer up the fat of the sin offering
25.note: tn Heb “And the fat of the sin offering he is to offer up.”

26.anchor: and the one who sent the goat away to Azazel
26.note: tn For “Azazel” see the note on v. 8 above.

27.anchor: The bull of the sin offering and the goat of the sin offering, whose blood was brought to make atonement in the holy place, must be brought outside the camp
27.note: tn Heb “he shall bring into from outside to the camp.”

27.anchor: and their hide, their flesh, and their dung must be burned up,
27.note: tn Heb “they shall burn with fire”; KJV “burn in the fire.” Because “to burn with fire” is redundant in contemporary English the present translation simply has “must be burned up.”

29.anchor: “This is to be a perpetual statute for you.
29.note: tn Heb “And it [feminine] shall be for you a perpetual statute.” Verse 34 begins with the same clause except for the missing demonstrative pronoun “this” here in v. 29. The LXX has “this” in both places and it suits the sense of the passage, although both the verb and the pronoun are sometimes missing in this clause elsewhere in the book (see, e.g., Lev 3:17).

29.anchor: In the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you must humble yourselves
29.note: tn Heb “you shall humble your souls.” The verb “to humble” here refers to various forms of self-denial, including but not limited to fasting (cf. Ps 35:13 and Isa 58:3, 10). The Mishnah (m. Yoma 8:1) lists abstentions from food and drink, bathing, using oil as an unguent to moisten the skin, wearing leather sandals, and sexual intercourse (cf. 2 Sam 12:16-17, 20; see the remarks in J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1054; B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 109; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 242).

29.anchor: and do no work of any kind,
29.note: tn Heb “and all work you shall not do.”

29.anchor: both the native citizen and the foreigner who resides
29.note: tn Heb “the native and the sojourner who sojourns.”

30.note: tn The phrase “from all your sins” could go with the previous clause as the verse is rendered here (see, e.g., B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 109, and J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1011), or it could go with the following clause (i.e., “you shall be clean from all your sins before the Lord”; see the MT accents as well as J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 221, and recent English versions, e.g., NASB, NIV, NRSV).

31.anchor: It is to be a Sabbath of complete rest for you, and you must humble yourselves.
31.note: tn See the note on v. 29 above.

32.anchor: “The priest who is anointed and ordained to act as high priest in place of his father
32.note: tn Heb “And the priest whom he shall anointed him and whom he shall fill his hand to act as priest under his father.” Imperfect active verbs are often used as passives (see, e.g., v. 27 above and the note on Lev 14:4).

33.anchor: and he is to purify
33.note: tn Heb “to atone” (also later in this verse); see the note on “purifying the holy place” in 16:20.

33.anchor: the Most Holy Place,
33.note: tn Heb “the sanctuary of the holy place.” Although this is the only place this expression occurs in the OT, it clearly refers to the innermost shrine behind the veil-canopy, where the ark of the covenant was located.

33.anchor: he is to purify the Meeting Tent and the altar,
33.note: tn Heb “and the tent of meeting and the alter he shall atone.” The repetition of the verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, “to atone”) at the beginning and end of the sequence appears to be strange, but the MT accents suggest that only “the Most Holy Place” goes with the verb at the beginning of the verse. Of course, the purging of “the Most Holy Place” has been the main emphasis of this chapter from the start (see vv. 2-3 and 11-17).

33.anchor: and he is to make atonement for
33.note: tn At this point in the verse the verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, “to make atonement”) takes its object with the preposition עַל (’al, “for”; literally, “upon”; contrast the first part of the verse and cf. the notes on Lev 1:4 and 16:20 above).

34.anchor: This is to be a perpetual statute for you
34.note: tn Heb “And this shall be for you to a statute of eternity” (cf. v. 29a above). cf. NASB “a permanent statute”; NIV “a lasting ordinance.”

34.anchor: to make atonement for the Israelites for
34.note: tn Heb “from”; see note on 4:26.

34.anchor: all their sins once a year.”
34.note: tn Heb “one [feminine] in the year.”

34.anchor: had commanded Moses.
34.note: tn The MT of Lev 16:34b reads literally, “and he did just as the Lord had commanded Moses.” This has been retained here in spite of the fact that it suggests that Aaron immediately performed the rituals outlined in Lev 16 (see, e.g., J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 224 and 243; J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:1059; note that Aaron was the one to whom Moses was to speak the regulations in this chapter, v. 2). The problem is that the chapter presents these procedures as regulations for “the tenth day of the seventh month” and calls for their fulfillment at that time (Lev 16:29; cf. Lev 23:26-32 and the remarks in P. J. Budd, Leviticus [NCBC], 237), not during the current (first) month (Exod 40:2; note also that they left Sinai in the second month, long before the next seventh month, Num 10:11). The LXX translates, “once in the year it shall be done as the Lord commanded Moses,” attaching “once in the year” to this clause rather than the former one, and rendering the verb as passive, “it shall be done” (cf. NAB, NIV, etc.). We have already observed the passive use of active verbs in this context (see the note on v. 32 above). The RSV (cf. also the NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT) translates, “And Moses did as the Lord commanded him,” ignoring the fact that the name Moses in the Hebrew text has the direct object indicator. Passive verbs, however, regularly take subjects with direct object indicators (see, e.g., v. 27 above). The NIV renders it “And it was done, as the Lord commanded Moses,” following the LXX passive translation. The NASB translates, “And just as the Lord had commanded Moses, so he did,” transposing the introductory verb to the end of the sentence and supplying “so” in order to make it fit the context.

3.anchor: Each of you must respect his mother and his father,
3.note: tn Heb “A man his mother and his father you [plural] shall fear.” The LXX, Syriac, Vulgate, and certain Targum mss reverse the order, “his father and his mother.” The term “fear” is subject to misunderstanding by the modern reader, so “respect” has been used in the translation. Cf. NAB, NRSV “revere”; NASB “reverence.”

4.anchor: Do not turn to idols,
4.note: sn Regarding the difficult etymology and meaning of the term for “idols” (אֱלִילִים, ’elilim), see B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 126; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 304; N. H. Snaith, Leviticus and Numbers (NBC), 89; and Judith M. Hadley, NIDOTTE 1:411. It appears to be a diminutive play on words with אֵל (’el, “god; God”) and, perhaps at the same time, recalls a common Semitic word for “worthless; weak; powerless; nothingness.” Snaith suggests a rendering of “worthless godlings.”

5.anchor: , you must sacrifice it so that it is accepted for you.
5.note: tn Heb “for your acceptance”; cf. NIV, NLT “it will be accepted on your behalf.”

6.anchor: It must be eaten on the day of your sacrifice and on the following day,
6.note: tn Heb “from the following day” (HALOT 572 s.v. מָחֳרָת 2.b).

6.anchor: but what is left over until the third day must be burned up.
6.note: tn Heb “shall be burned with fire”; KJV “shall be burnt in the fire.” Because “to burn with fire” is redundant in contemporary English the present translation simply has “must be burned up.”

7.anchor: If, however, it is eaten
7.note: tn Heb “And if being eaten [infinitive absolute] it is eaten [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

7.anchor: on the third day, it is spoiled,
7.note: tn Or “desecrated,” or “defiled,” or “forbidden.” For this difficult term see J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:422, on Lev 7:18.

8.anchor: and the one who eats it will bear his punishment for iniquity
8.note: tn See the note on Lev 17:16 above.

8.anchor: because he has profaned
8.note: sn Regarding “profaned,” see the note on Lev 10:10 above.
tn Heb “the holiness of the Lord.”

8.anchor: That person will be cut off from his people.
8.note: sn On the “cut off” penalty see the note on Lev 7:20.

9.anchor: “‘When you gather in the harvest
9.note: tn Heb “And in your harvesting the harvest.”

9.anchor: of your land, you must not completely harvest the corner of your field,
9.note: tn Heb “you shall not complete the corner of your field to harvest.”

10.anchor: You must not pick your vineyard bare,
10.note: tn Heb “And you shall not deal severely with your vineyard.”

11.anchor: “‘You must not steal, you must not tell lies, and you must not deal falsely with your fellow citizen.
11.note: tn Heb “you shall not deal falsely a man with his fellow citizen.”

12.anchor: You must not swear falsely
12.note: tn Heb “And you shall not swear to the falsehood.”

12.anchor: in my name, so that you do not profane
12.note: tn Heb “and you shall not profane”; NAB “thus profaning.”

13.anchor: You must not oppress your neighbor or commit robbery against him.
13.note: tn Heb “You shall not oppress your neighbor and you shall not rob.”

13.anchor: You must not withhold
13.note: tn Heb “hold back with you”; perhaps “hold back for yourself” (cf. NRSV “keep for yourself”).

14.anchor: You must not curse a deaf person or put a stumbling block in front of a blind person.
14.note: tn Heb “You shall not curse a deaf [person] and before a blind [person] you shall not put a stumbling block.”

14.anchor: You must fear
14.note: tn Heb “And you shall fear.” Many English versions (e.g., KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV) regard the Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) as adversative in force here (“but”).

15.anchor: “‘You
15.note: tc Smr has the singular rather than the plural “you” of the MT, which brings this verb form into line with the ones surrounding it.

15.anchor: must not deal unjustly in judgment:
15.note: tn Heb “You shall not do injustice in judgment” (NASB similar); cf. NIV “do not pervert justice.”

15.anchor: you must neither show partiality to the poor nor honor the rich.
15.note: tn Heb “You shall not lift up faces of poor [people] and you shall not honor faces of great.”

15.anchor: You must judge your fellow citizen fairly.
15.note: tn Heb “In righteousness you shall judge your fellow citizen.”

16.anchor: You must not go about as a slanderer among your people.
16.note: tn The term רָכִיל (rakhil) is traditionally rendered “slanderer” here (so NASB, NIV, NRSV; see also J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 304, 316), but the exact meaning is uncertain (see the discussion in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 129). It is sometimes related to I רָכַל (“to go about as a trader [or “merchant”]”; BDB 940 s.v. רָכַל), and taken to refer to cutthroat business dealings, but there may be a II רָכַל, the meaning of which is dubious (HALOT 1237 s.v. II *רכל). Some would render it “to go about as a spy.”

16.anchor: You must not stand idly by when your neighbor’s life is at stake.
16.note: tn Heb “You shall not stand on the blood of your neighbor.” This part of the verse is also difficult to interpret. The rendering here suggests that one will not allow a neighbor to be victimized, whether in court (cf. v. 15) or in any other situation (see the discussion in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 129).

17.anchor: You must not hate your brother in your heart. You must surely reprove your fellow citizen so that you do not incur sin on account of him.
17.note: tn Heb “and you will not lift up on him sin.” The meaning of the line is somewhat obscure. It means either (1) that one should rebuke one’s neighbor when he sins lest one also becomes guilty, which is the way it is rendered here (see NIV, NRSV, NEB, JB; see also B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 129-30, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 303, and the discussion on pp. 316-17), or (2) one may rebuke one’s neighbor without incurring sin just as long as he does not hate him in his heart (see the first part of the verse; cf. NASB, NAB).

18.anchor: You must not take vengeance or bear a grudge
18.note: tn Heb “and you shall not retain [anger?].” This line seems to refer to the retaining or maintaining of some vengeful feelings toward someone. Compare the combination of the same terms for taking vengeance and maintaining wrath against enemies in Nahum 1:2 (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 305).

18.anchor: against the children of your people, but you must love your neighbor as yourself.
18.note: sn Some scholars make a distinction between the verb אָהַב (’ahav, “to love”) with the direct object and the more unusual construction with the preposition לְ (lamed) as it is here and in Lev 19:34 and 2 Chr 19:2 only. If there is a distinction, the construction here probably calls for direct and helpful action toward one’s neighbor (see the discussion in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 305, and esp. 317-18). Such love stands in contrast to taking vengeance or bearing a grudge against someone and, in NT terms, amounts to fulfilling the so-called “golden rule” (Matt 7:12).

19.anchor: You must keep my statutes. You must not allow two different kinds of your animals to breed,
19.note: tn Heb “Your animals, you shall not cross-breed two different kinds.”

19.anchor: you must not sow your field with two different kinds of seed, and you must not wear
19.note: tn Heb “you shall not cause to go up on you.”

19.anchor: a garment made of two different kinds of fabric.
19.note: sn Cf. Deut 22:11 where the Hebrew term translated “two different kinds” (כִּלְאַיִם, kil’ayim) refers to a mixture of linen and wool woven together in a garment.

20.anchor: “‘When a man has sexual intercourse with a woman,
20.note: tn Heb “And a man when he lies with a woman the lying of seed.”

20.anchor: although she is a slave woman designated for another man and she has not yet been ransomed, or freedom has not been granted to her, there will be an obligation to pay compensation.
20.note: sn That is, the woman had previously been assigned for marriage to another man but the marriage deal had not yet been consummated. In the meantime, the woman has lost her virginity and has, therefore, lost part of her value to the master in the sale to the man for whom she had been designated. Compensation was, therefore, required (see the explanation in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 130-31).

21.anchor: at the entrance of the Meeting Tent, a guilt offering ram,
21.note: sn On the guilt offering see the note on Lev 5:15 above.

22.anchor: for his sin that he has committed,
22.note: tn Heb “on his sin which he has sinned.”

22.anchor: and he will be forgiven
22.note: tn Heb “there shall be forgiveness to him” or “it shall be forgiven to him.”

22.anchor: of his sin
22.note: tn Heb “from his sin.”

23.anchor: “‘When you enter the land and plant any fruit tree,
23.note: tn Heb “tree of food”; KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV “trees for food.”

23.anchor: you must consider its fruit to be forbidden.
23.note: tn Heb “you shall circumcise its fruit [as] its foreskin,” taking the fruit to be that which is to be removed and, therefore, forbidden. Since the fruit is uncircumcised it is forbidden (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 306, and esp. B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 131-32).

23.anchor: Three years it will be forbidden to you;
23.note: tn Heb “it shall be to you uncircumcised.”

24.anchor: In the fourth year all its fruit will be holy, praise offerings
24.note: tn See B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 132, where the translation reads “set aside for jubilation”; a special celebration before the Lord.

25.anchor: Then in the fifth year you may eat its fruit to add its produce to your harvest.
25.note: tn Heb “to add to you its produce.” The rendering here assumes that the point of this clause is simply that finally being allowed to eat the fruit in the fifth year adds the fruit of the tree to their harvest. Some take the verb to be from אָסַף (’asaf, “to gather”) rather than יָסַף (yasaf, “to add; to increase”), rendering the verse, “to gather to you the produce” (E. S. Gerstenberger, Leviticus [OTL], 260, and see the versions referenced in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 306). Others take it to mean that by following the regulations given previously they will honor the Lord so that the Lord will cause the trees to increase the amount of fruit they would normally produce (Hartley, 303, 306; cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

26.anchor: “‘You must not eat anything with the blood still in it.
26.note: tn Heb “You shall not eat on the blood.” See the extensive remarks in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 319-20, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 132-33. The LXX has “on the mountains,” suggesting that this is a prohibition against illegitimate places and occasions of worship, not the eating of blood.

26.anchor: You must not practice either divination or soothsaying.
26.note: tn Heb “You shall not practice divination and you shall not practice soothsaying”; cf. NRSV “practice augury or witchcraft.” For suggestions regarding the practices involved see B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 133, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 320.

27.anchor: You must not round off the corners of the hair on your head or ruin the corners of your beard.
27.note: tc Heb “and you [singular] shall not ruin the corner of your [singular] beard.” Smr, LXX, Syriac, and Tg. Ps.-J. have the plural pronouns (i.e., “you” and “your” plural) rather than the singular of the MT.

28.anchor: You must not slash your body for a dead person
28.note: tn Heb “And slash for the soul you shall not give.” The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “soul, person, life”) can sometimes refer to a “dead person” (cf. Lev 21:1, 5; 22:5). See J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 306, 320-21.

28.anchor: or incise a tattoo on yourself.
28.note: tn Heb “and a writing of incision you shall not give in you.”

29.anchor: Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute,
29.note: tn Heb “to make her practice harlotry.” Some recent English versions regard this as religious or temple prostitution (cf. TEV, CEV).

29.anchor: so that the land does not practice prostitution and become full of lewdness.
29.note: tn Heb “and the land become full of lewdness.” Regarding the term “lewdness,” see the note on Lev 18:17 above.

31.anchor: Do not turn to the spirits of the dead and do not seek familiar spirits
31.note: sn The prohibition here concerns those who would seek special knowledge through the spirits of the dead, whether the dead in general or dead relatives in particular (i.e., familiar spirits; see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 321, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 134). Cf. Lev 20:6 below.

33.anchor: When a foreigner resides
33.note: tn Heb “And when a sojourner sojourns.”

34.anchor: The foreigner who resides with you must be to you like a native citizen among you; so
34.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

35.anchor: You must not do injustice in the regulation of measures, whether of length, weight, or volume.
35.note: tn That is, liquid capacity (HALOT 640 s.v. מְשׂוּרָה). Cf. ASV, NIV, NRSV, TEV “quantity”; NAB, NASB “capacity.”

36.note: tn Heb “balances of righteousness,” and so throughout this sentence.

36.anchor: honest weights, an honest ephah, and an honest hin.
36.note: sn An ephah is a dry measure which measures about four gallons, or perhaps one third of a bushel, while a hin is a liquid measure of about 3.6 liters (= approximately 1 quart).

37.anchor: You must be sure to obey all my statutes and regulations.
37.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep all my statutes and all my regulations and you shall do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 22:31).

1.anchor: said to Moses: “Say to the priests, the sons of Aaron – say to them, ‘For a dead person
1.note: tn The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “soul, person, life”) can sometimes refer to a “dead person” (cf. Lev 19:28 above and the literature cited there).

1.anchor: no priest
1.note: tn Heb “no one,” but “priest” has been used in the translation to clarify that these restrictions are limited to the priests, not to the Israelites in general (note the introductory formula, “say to the priests, the sons of Aaron”).

1.anchor: is to defile himself among his people,
1.note: tc The MT has “in his peoples,” but Smr, LXX, Syriac, Targum, and Tg. Ps.-J. have “in his people,” referring to the Israelites as a whole.

2.anchor: except for his close relative who is near to him:
2.note: tn Heb “except for his flesh, the one near to him.”

3.anchor: and his virgin sister who is near to him,
3.note: tn Cf. v. 2a.

4.anchor: He must not defile himself as a husband among his people so as to profane himself.
4.note: tn Heb “He shall not defile himself a husband in his peoples, to profane himself.” The meaning of the line is disputed, but it appears to prohibit a priest from burying any relative by marriage (as opposed to the blood relatives of vv. 2-3), including his wife (compare B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 142-43 with J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 343, 348).

5.anchor: Priests
5.note: tn Heb “they”; the referent (priests, see the beginning of v. 1) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

5.anchor: must not have a bald spot shaved on their head, they must not shave the corner of their beard, and they must not cut slashes in their body.
5.note: tn Heb “and in their body they shall not [cut] slash[es]” (cf. Lev 19:28). The context connects these sorts of mutilations with mourning rites (cf. Lev 19:27-28 above).

6.anchor: “‘They must be holy to their God, and they must not profane
6.note: sn Regarding “profane,” see the note on Lev 10:10 above.

6.anchor: ’s gifts,
6.note: sn Regarding the Hebrew term for “gifts,” see the note on Lev 1:9 above (cf. also 3:11 and 16 in combination with the word for “food” that follows in the next phrase here).

6.anchor: the food of their God. Therefore they must be holy.
6.note: tc Smr and all early versions have the plural adjective “holy” rather than the MT singular noun “holiness.”

7.anchor: They must not take a wife defiled by prostitution,
7.note: tn Heb “A wife harlot and profaned they shall not take.” The structure of the verse (e.g., “wife” at the beginning of the two main clauses) suggests that “harlot and profaned” constitutes a hendiadys, meaning “a wife defiled by harlotry” (see the explanation in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 143, as opposed to that in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 343, 348; cf. v. 14 below). Cf. NASB “a woman who is profaned by harlotry.”

7.anchor: nor are they to take a wife divorced from her husband,
7.note: sn For a helpful discussion of divorce in general and as it relates to this passage see B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 143-44.

7.anchor: for the priest
7.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the priest) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

7.anchor: is holy to his God.
7.note: tn The pronoun “he” in this clause refers to the priest, not the former husband of the divorced woman.

8.anchor: who sanctifies you all,
8.note: tn The three previous second person references in this verse are all singular, but this reference is plural. By adding “all” this grammatical distinction is preserved in the translation.

9.anchor: If a daughter of a priest profanes herself by engaging in prostitution, she is profaning her father. She must be burned to death.
9.note: tn See the note on “burned to death” in 20:14.

10.anchor: “‘The high
10.note: tn The adjective “high” has been supplied in the translation for clarity, as in many English versions.

10.anchor: priest – who is greater than his brothers, on whose head the anointing oil is poured, who has been ordained
10.note: tn Heb “and he has filled his hand.” For this expression see the note on Lev 8:33.

10.anchor: to wear the priestly garments – must neither dishevel the hair of his head nor tear his garments.
10.note: tn Regarding these signs of mourning see the note on Lev 10:6. His head had been anointed (v. 10a) so it must not be unkempt (v. 10b), and his garments were special priestly garments (v. 10a) so he must not tear them (v. 10b). In the translation “garments” has been employed rather than “clothes” to suggest that the special priestly garments are referred to here; cf. NRSV “nor tear his vestments.”

11.anchor: He must not go where there is any dead person;
11.note: tc Although the MT has “persons” (plural), the LXX and Syriac have the singular “person” corresponding to the singular adjectival participle “dead” (cf. also Num 6:6).

12.anchor: He must not go out from the sanctuary and must not profane
12.note: sn Regarding “profane,” see the note on Lev 10:10 above.

13.anchor: He must take a wife who is a virgin.
13.note: tn Heb “And he, a wife in her virginity he shall take.”

14.anchor: He must not marry
14.note: tn Heb “take.” In context this means “take as wife,” i.e., “marry.”

14.anchor: a widow, a divorced woman, or one profaned by prostitution; he may only take a virgin from his people
14.note: tc The MT has literally, “from his peoples,” but Smr, LXX, Syriac, Targum, and Tg. Ps.-J. have “from his people,” referring to the Israelites as a whole.

15.anchor: He must not profane his children among his people,
15.note: tc The MT has literally, “in his peoples,” but Smr, LXX, Syriac, Targum, and Tg. Ps.-J. have “in his people,” referring to the Israelites as a whole.

17.anchor: “Tell Aaron, ‘No man from your descendants throughout their generations
17.note: tn Heb “to their generations.”

17.anchor: who has a physical flaw
17.note: tn Heb “who in him is a flaw”; cf. KJV, ASV “any blemish”; NASB, NIV “a defect.” The rendering “physical flaw” is used to refer to any birth defect or physical injury of the kind described in the following verses (cf. the same Hebrew word also in Lev 24:19-20). The same term is used for “flawed” animals, which must not be offered to the Lord in Lev 22:20-25.

18.anchor: Certainly
18.note: tn The particle כִּי (ki) in this context is asseverative, indicating absolutely certainty (GKC 498 §159.ee).

18.anchor: no man who has a physical flaw is to approach: a blind man, or one who is lame, or one with a slit nose,
18.note: tn Lexically, the Hebrew term חָרֻם (kharum) seems to refer to a split nose or perhaps any number of other facial defects (HALOT 354 s.v. II חרם qal; cf. G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 292, n. 7); cf. KJV, ASV “a flat nose”; NASB “a disfigured face.” The NJPS translation is “a limb too short” as a balance to the following term which means “extended, raised,” and apparently refers to “a limb too long” (see the explanation in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 146).

19.anchor: or a man who has had a broken leg or arm,
19.note: tn Heb “who there is in him a broken leg or a broken arm,” or perhaps “broken foot or broken hand.” The Hebrew term רֶגֶל (regel) is commonly rendered “foot,” but it can also refer to the “leg,” and the Hebrew יָד (yad) is most often translated “hand,” but can also refer to the “[fore]arm” (as opposed to כַּף, kaf, “palm of the hand” or “hand”). See HALOT 386 s.v. יָד and 1184 s.v. רֶגֶל respectively (cf. the NJPS translation). In this context, these terms probably apply to any part of the limb that was broken, including hand and the foot. B. A. Levine (Leviticus [JPSTC], 146) points out that such injuries often did not heal properly in antiquity because they were not properly set and, therefore, remained a “physical flaw” permanently.

20.anchor: or a hunchback, or a dwarf,
20.note: tn Heb “thin”; cf. NAB “weakly.” This could refer to either an exceptionally small (i.e., dwarfed) man (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 146) or perhaps one with a “withered limb” (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 342, 344).

20.anchor: or one with a spot in his eye,
20.note: tn The term rendered “spot” derives from a root meaning “mixed” or “confused” (cf. NAB “walleyed”). It apparently refers to any kind of marked flaw in the eye that can be seen by others. Smr, Syriac, Tg. Onq., and Tg. Ps.-J. have plural “his eyes.”

20.anchor: or a festering eruption, or a feverish rash,
20.note: tn The exact meaning and medical reference of the terms rendered “festering eruption” and “feverish rash” is unknown, but see the translations and remarks in B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 146; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 342, 344, 349-50; and R. K. Harrison, NIDOTTE 1:890 and 2:461.

21.anchor: No man from the descendants of Aaron the priest who has a physical flaw may step forward
21.note: tn Or “shall approach” (see HALOT 670 s.v. נגשׁ).

23.anchor: but he must not go into the veil-canopy
23.note: sn See the note on Lev 16:2 for the rendering “veil-canopy.”

23.anchor: or step forward to the altar because he has a physical flaw. Thus
23.note: tn Heb “And.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

24.anchor: So
24.note: tn Heb “And.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) introduces a concluding statement for all the preceding material.

24.anchor: Moses spoke these things
24.note: tn The words “these things” are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation for clarity.

2.anchor: “You are to say to the Israelites, ‘Any man from the Israelites or from the foreigners who reside in Israel
2.note: tn Heb “or from the sojourner who sojourns”; NAB “an alien residing in Israel.”

2.anchor: who gives any of his children
2.note: tn Heb “his seed” (so KJV, ASV); likewise in vv. 3-4.

2.anchor: to Molech
2.note: tn Regarding Molech and Molech worship see the note on Lev 18:21.

2.anchor: must be put to death; the people of the land must pelt him with stones.
2.note: tn This is not the most frequently-used Hebrew verb for stoning (see instead סָקַל, saqal), but a word that refers to the action of throwing, slinging, or pelting someone with stones (רָגָם, ragam; see HALOT 1187 s.v. רגם qal.a, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 136).

3.anchor: I myself will set my face
3.note: tn Heb “And I, I shall give my faces.”

3.anchor: against that man and cut him off from the midst of his people,
3.note: sn On the “cut off” penalty see the notes on Lev 7:20 and 17:4.

3.anchor: because he has given some of his children to Molech and thereby defiled my sanctuary and profaned my holy name.
3.note: tn Heb “for the sake of defiling my sanctuary and to profane my holy name.”

4.anchor: If, however, the people of the land shut their eyes
4.note: tn Heb “And if shutting [infinitive absolute] they shut [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

4.anchor: to that man
4.note: tn Heb “from that man” (so ASV); NASB “disregard that man.”

5.anchor: I myself will set my face against that man and his clan. I will cut off from the midst of their people both him and all who follow after him in spiritual prostitution,
5.note: tn The adjective “spiritual” has been supplied in the translation to clarify that this is not a reference to literal prostitution, but figuratively compares idolatry to prostitution.

5.anchor: to commit prostitution by worshiping Molech.
5.note: tn Heb “to commit harlotry after Molech.” The translation employs “worshiping” here for clarity (cf. NAB, NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT). On the “cut off” penalty see the note on Lev 7:20.

5.anchor: to commit prostitution by worshiping Molech.
5.note: sn For structure and coherence in Lev 20:6-27 see the note on v. 27 below.

6.anchor: “‘The person who turns to the spirits of the dead and familiar spirits
6.note: tn See the note on the phrase “familiar spirits” in Lev 19:31 above.

6.anchor: to commit prostitution by going after them, I will set my face
6.note: tn Heb “I will give my faces.”

8.anchor: You must be sure to obey my statutes.
8.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep my statutes and you shall do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 22:31, etc.).

8.anchor: who sanctifies you.
8.note: sn Compare the regulations in Lev 18:6-23.

9.anchor: “‘If anyone
9.note: tn Heb “If a man a man who.”

9.anchor: curses his father and mother
9.note: tn Heb “makes light of his father and his mother.” Almost all English versions render this as some variation of “curses his father or mother.”

9.anchor: he must be put to death. He has cursed his father and mother; his blood guilt is on himself.
9.note: tn Heb “his blood [plural] is in him.” Cf. NAB “he has forfeited his life”; TEV “is responsible for his own death.”sn The rendering “blood guilt” refers to the fact that the shedding of blood brings guilt on those who shed it illegitimately (even the blood of animals shed illegitimately, Lev 17:4; cf. the background of Gen 4:10-11). If the community performs a legitimate execution, however, the blood guilt rests on the person who has been legitimately executed (see the remarks and literature cited in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 328).

10.anchor: If a man
10.note: tn Heb “And a man who.” The syntax here and at the beginning of the following verses elliptically mirrors that of v. 9, which justifies the rendering as a conditional clause.

10.anchor: commits adultery with his neighbor’s wife,
10.note: tc The reading of the LXX minuscule mss has been followed here (see the BHS footnote a-a). The MT has a dittography, repeating “a man who commits adultery with the wife of” (see the explanation in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 328). The duplication found in the MT is reflected in some English versions, e.g., KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV.

11.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with his father’s wife, he has exposed his father’s nakedness.
11.note: sn See the note on Lev 18:7 above.

11.anchor: Both of them must be put to death; their blood guilt is on themselves.
11.note: tn See the note on v. 9 above.

12.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with his daughter-in-law, both of them must be put to death. They have committed perversion;
12.note: tn The Hebrew term תֶּבֶל (tevel, “perversion”) derives from the verb “to mix; to confuse” (cf. KJV, ASV “they have wrought confusion”).

13.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with a male as one has sexual intercourse with a woman,
13.note: tn Heb “[as the] lyings of a woman.” The specific reference here is to homosexual intercourse between males.

14.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with both a woman and her mother,
14.note: tn Heb “And a man who takes a woman and her mother.” The Hebrew verb “to take” in this context means “to engage in sexual intercourse.”

14.anchor: it is lewdness.
14.note: tn Regarding “lewdness,” see the note on Lev 18:17 above.

14.anchor: Both he and they must be burned to death,
14.note: tn Heb “in fire they shall burn him and them.” The active plural verb sometimes requires a passive translation (GKC 460 §144.f, g), esp. when no active plural subject has been expressed in the context. The present translation specifies “burned to death” because the traditional rendering “burnt with fire” (KJV, ASV; NASB “burned with fire”) could be understood to mean “branded” or otherwise burned, but not fatally.

15.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse
15.note: tn See the note on Lev 18:20 above.

16.anchor: If a woman approaches any animal to have sexual intercourse with it,
16.note: tn Heb “to copulate with it” (cf. Lev 20:16).

17.anchor: “‘If a man has sexual intercourse with
17.note: tn Heb “takes.” The verb “to take” in this context means “to engage in sexual intercourse,” though some English versions translate it as “marry” (e.g., NIV, NCV, TEV, CEV).

17.anchor: his sister, whether the daughter of his father or his mother, so that he sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace. They must be cut off in the sight of the children of their people.
17.note: tn Regarding the “cut off” penalty, see the note on Lev 7:20.

17.anchor: He has exposed his sister’s nakedness; he will bear his punishment for iniquity.
17.note: tn See the note on Lev 17:16 above.

18.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with a menstruating woman and uncovers her nakedness, he has laid bare her fountain of blood and she has exposed the fountain of her blood, so both of them
18.note: tn Heb “and the two of them.”

19.anchor: You must not expose the nakedness of your mother’s sister and your father’s sister, for such a person has laid bare his own close relative.
19.note: tn Heb “his flesh.”

19.anchor: They must bear their punishment for iniquity.
19.note: tn See the note on Lev 17:16 above.

21.anchor: If a man has sexual intercourse with
21.note: tn Heb “takes.” The verb “to take” in this context means “to engage in sexual intercourse.”

21.anchor: his brother’s wife, it is indecency. He has exposed his brother’s nakedness;
21.note: sn See the note on Lev 18:7 above.

22.anchor: “‘You must be sure to obey all my statutes and regulations,
22.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep all my statutes and all my regulations and you shall do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 22:31, etc.).

22.anchor: so that
22.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

23.anchor: You must not walk in the statutes of the nation
23.note: tc One medieval Hebrew ms, Smr, and all the major ancient versions have the plural “nations.” Some English versions retain the singular (e.g., KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV); others have the plural “nations” (e.g., NAB, NIV) and still others translate as “people” (e.g., TEV, NLT).

24.anchor: your God who has set you apart from the other peoples.
24.note: tc Here and with the same phrase in v. 26, the LXX adds “all,” resulting in the reading “all the peoples.”

25.anchor: Therefore you must distinguish
25.note: tn Heb “And you shall distinguish.” The verb is the same as “set apart” at the end of the previous verse. The fact that God had “set them apart” from the other peoples roundabout them called for them to “distinguish between” the clean and the unclean, etc.

25.anchor: between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean, and you must not make yourselves detestable by means of an animal or bird or anything that creeps on the ground – creatures
25.note: tn The word “creatures” has been supplied in the translation to make it clear that the following relative clause modifies the animal, bird, or creeping thing mentioned earlier, and not the ground itself.

25.anchor: I have distinguished for you as unclean.
25.note: tc The MT has “to defile,” but Smr, LXX, and Syriac have “to uncleanness.”

27.anchor: “‘A man or woman who
27.note: tc Smr, LXX, Syriac, and some Targum mss have the relative pronoun אֲשֶׁר (’asher, “who, which”), rather than the MT’s כִּי (ki, “for, because, that”).

27.anchor: has in them a spirit of the dead or a familiar spirit
27.note: tn See the note on the phrase “familiar spirit” in Lev 19:31 above.

27.anchor: must be put to death. They must pelt them with stones;
27.note: tn This is not the most frequently-used Hebrew verb for stoning, but a word that refers to the action of throwing, slinging, or pelting someone with stones (see the note on v. 2 above). Smr and LXX have “you [plural] shall pelt them with stones.” sn At first glance Lev 20:27 appears to be out of place but, on closer examination, one could argue that it constitutes the back side of an envelope around the case laws in 20:9-21, with Lev 20:6 forming the front of the envelope (note also that execution of mediums and spiritists by stoning in v. 27 is not explicitly stated in v. 6). This creates a chiastic structure: prohibition against mediums and spiritists (vv. 6 and 27), variations of the holiness formula (vv. 7 and 25-26), and exhortations to obey the Lord’s statutes (and judgments; vv. 8 and 22-24). Again, in the middle are the case laws (vv. 9-21).

2.anchor: “Tell Aaron and his sons that they must deal respectfully with the holy offerings
2.note: tn Heb “holy things,” which means the “holy offerings” in this context, as the following verses show. The referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.

2.anchor: of the Israelites, which they consecrate to me, so that they do not profane my holy name.
2.note: tn Heb “from the holy things of the sons of Israel, and they shall not profane my holy name, which they are consecrating to me.” The latter (relative) clause applies to the “the holy things of the sons of Israel” (the first clause), not the Lord’s name (i.e., the immediately preceding clause). The clause order in the translation has been rearranged to indicate this.

3.anchor: Say to them, ‘Throughout your generations,
3.note: tn Heb “To your generations.”

3.anchor: if any man from all your descendants approaches the holy offerings which the Israelites consecrate
3.note: tn The Piel (v. 2) and Hiphil (v. 3) forms of the verb קָדַשׁ (qadash) appear to be interchangeable in this context. Both mean “to consecrate” (Heb “make holy [or “sacred”]”).

3.anchor: while he is impure,
3.note: tn Heb “and his impurity [is] on him”; NIV “is ceremonially unclean”; NAB, NRSV “while he is in a state of uncleanness.”

3.anchor: that person must be cut off from before me.
3.note: sn Regarding the “cut off” penalty, see the note on Lev 7:20. Cf. the interpretive translation of TEV “he can never again serve at the altar.”

4.anchor: No man
4.note: tn Heb “Man man.” The reduplication is a way of saying “any man” (cf. Lev 15:2; 17:3, etc.), but with a negative command it means “No man” (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 147).

4.anchor: from the descendants of Aaron who is diseased or has a discharge
4.note: sn The diseases and discharges mentioned here are those described in Lev 13-15.

4.anchor: may eat the holy offerings until he becomes clean. The one
4.note: tn Heb “And the one.”

4.anchor: who touches anything made unclean by contact with a dead person,
4.note: tn Heb “in all unclean of a person/soul”; for the Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) meaning “a [dead] person,” see the note on Lev 19:28.

4.anchor: or a man who has a seminal emission,
4.note: tn Heb “or a man who goes out from him a lying of seed.”

5.anchor: or a man who touches a swarming thing by which he becomes unclean,
5.note: tn Heb “which there shall be uncleanness to him.”

5.anchor: or touches a person
5.note: tn The Hebrew term for “person” here is אָדָם (adam, “human being”), which could either a male or a female person.

5.anchor: by which he becomes unclean, whatever that person’s impurity
5.note: tn Heb “to all his impurity.” The phrase refers to the impurity of the person whom the man touches to become unclean (see the previous clause). To clarify this, the translation uses “that person’s” rather than “his.”

6.anchor: the person who touches any of these
6.note: sn The phrase “any of these” refers back to the unclean things touched in vv. 4b-5.

8.anchor: He must not eat an animal that has died of natural causes
8.note: tn Heb “a carcass,” referring to the carcass of an animal that has died on its own, not the carcass of an animal slaughtered for sacrifice or killed by wild beasts. This has been clarified in the translation by supplying the phrase “of natural causes”; cf. NAB “that has died of itself”; TEV “that has died a natural death.”

9.anchor: They must keep my charge so that they do not incur sin on account of it
9.note: tn Heb “and they will not lift up on it sin.” The pronoun “it” (masculine) apparently refers to any item of food that belongs to the category of “holy offerings” (see above).

9.anchor: and therefore die
9.note: tn Heb “and die in it.”

10.anchor: “‘No lay person
10.note: tn Heb “No stranger” (so KJV, ASV), which refers here to anyone other than the Aaronic priests. Some English versions reverse the negation and state positively: NIV “No one outside a priest’s family”; NRSV “Only a member of a priestly family”; CEV “Only you priests and your families.”

10.anchor: may eat anything holy. Neither a priest’s lodger
10.note: tn Heb “A resident [תּוֹשָׁב (toshav) from יָשַׁב (yashav, “to dwell, to reside”)] of a priest.” The meaning of the term is uncertain. It could refer to a “guest” (NIV) or perhaps “bound servant” (NRSV; see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 149). In the translation “lodger” was used instead of “boarder” precisely because a boarder would be provided meals with his lodging, the very issue at stake here.

11.anchor: but if a priest buys a person with his own money,
11.note: tn Heb “and a priest, if he buys a person, the property of his silver.”

11.anchor: that person
11.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the person whom the priest has purchased) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: may eat the holy offerings,
11.note: tn Heb “eat it”; the referent (the holy offerings) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: and those born in the priest’s
11.note: tn Heb “his”; the referent (the priest) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: own house may eat his food.
11.note: tn Heb “and the [slave] born of his house, they shall eat in his food.” The LXX, Syriac, Tg. Onq., Tg. Ps.-J., and some mss of Smr have plural “ones born,” which matches the following plural “they” pronoun and the plural form of the verb.

12.anchor: If a priest’s daughter marries a lay person,
12.note: tn Heb “And a daughter of a priest, if she is to a man, a stranger” (cf. the note on v. 10 above).

12.anchor: she may not eat the holy contribution offerings,
12.note: tn Heb “she in the contribution of the holy offerings shall not eat.” For “contribution [offering]” see the note on Lev 7:14 and the literature cited there. Cf. NCV “the holy offerings”; TEV, NLT “the sacred offerings.”

13.anchor: but if a priest’s daughter is a widow or divorced, and she has no children so that she returns to live in
13.note: tn Heb “to”; the words “live in” have been supplied in the translation for clarity.

13.anchor: her father’s house as in her youth,
13.note: tn Heb “and seed there is not to her and she returns to the house of her father as her youth.” The mention of having “no children” appears to imply that her children, if she had any, should support her; this is made explicit by NLT’s “and has no children to support her.”

14.anchor: “‘If a man eats a holy offering by mistake,
14.note: tn Heb “And a man, if he eats a holy thing in error” (see the Lev 4:2 not on “straying,” which is the term rendered “by mistake” here).

14.anchor: he must add one fifth to it and give the holy offering to the priest.
14.note: sn When a person trespassed in regard to something sacred to the Lord, reparation was to be made for the trespass, involving restitution of that which was violated plus one fifth of its value as a fine. It is possible that the restoration of the offering and the additional one fifth of its value were made as a monetary payment (see, e.g., B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 150). See the regulations for the “guilt offering” in Lev 5:16; 6:5 [5:24 HT] and the notes there.

15.anchor: They
15.note: tn Contextually, “They” could refer either to the people (v. 14a; cf. NRSV “No one”) or the priests (v. 14b; cf. NIV “The priests”), but the latter seems more likely (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 356, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 150). The priests were responsible to see that the portions of the offerings that were to be consumed by the priests as prebends did not become accessible to the people. Mistakes in this matter (cf. v. 14) would bring “guilt” on the people, requiring punishment (v. 16).

15.anchor: must not profane the holy offerings which the Israelites contribute
15.note: tn The Hebrew verb הֵרִים (herim, rendered “contribute” here) is commonly used for setting aside portions of an offering (see, e.g., Lev 4:8-10 and R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 4:335-36).

tn Heb “the holy offerings of the sons of Israel which they contribute to the Lord.” The subject “they” here refers to the Israelites (“the sons of Israel”) which is the most immediate antecedent. To make this clear, the present translation has “the holy offerings which the Israelites contribute to the Lord.”

16.anchor: and so cause them to incur a penalty for guilt
