16.note: tn Heb “iniquity of guilt”; NASB “cause them to bear punishment for guilt.” The Hebrew word עָוֹן (’avon, “iniquity”) can designate either acts of iniquity or the penalty (i.e., punishment) for such acts.

16.anchor: when they eat their holy offerings,
16.note: sn That is, when the lay people eat portions of offerings that should have been eaten only by priests and those who belonged to priestly households.

18.anchor: “Speak to Aaron, his sons, and all the Israelites and tell them, ‘When any man
18.note: tn Heb “Man, man.” The reduplication is a way of saying “any man” (cf. Lev 15:2; 17:3, etc.; see the distributive repetition of the noun in GKC 395-96 §123.c).

18.anchor: from the house of Israel or from the foreigners in Israel
18.note: tn Heb “and from the foreigner [singular] in Israel.” Some medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate add “who resides” after “foreigner”: “the foreigner who resides in Israel” (cf., e.g., Lev 20:2 above).

19.anchor: if it is to be acceptable for your benefit
19.note: tn Heb “for your acceptance.” See Lev 1:3-4 above and the notes there.

20.anchor: You must not present anything that has a flaw,
20.note: tn Heb “all which in it [is] a flaw.” Note that the same term is used for physical flaws of people in Lev 21:17-24. Cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV “blemish”; NASB, NIV, TEV “defect”; NLT “with physical defects.”

20.anchor: because it will not be acceptable for your benefit.
20.note: tn Heb “not for acceptance shall it be for you”; NIV “it will not be accepted on your behalf” (NRSV and NLT both similar).

21.anchor: for a special votive offering
21.note: tn The meaning of the expression לְפַלֵּא־נֶדֶר (lÿfalle’-neder) rendered here “for a special votive offering” is much debated. Some take it as an expression for fulfilling a vow, “to fulfill a vow” (e.g., HALOT 927-28 s.v. פלא piel and NASB; cf. NAB, NRSV “in fulfillment of a vow”) or, alternatively, “to make a vow” or “for making a vow” (HALOT 928 s.v. פלא piel [II פלא]). Perhaps it refers to the making a special vow, from the verb פָלַא (pala’, “to be wonderful, to be remarkable”); cf. J. Milgrom, Numbers (JPSTC), 44. B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 151 and 193, suggests that this is a special term for “setting aside a votive offering” (related to פָלָה [palah, “to set aside”]). In general, the point of the expression seems to be that this sacrifice arises as a special gift to God out of special circumstances in the life of the worshiper.

21.anchor: or for a freewill offering from the herd or the flock, it must be flawless to be acceptable;
21.note: tn Heb “for acceptance”; NAB “if it is to find acceptance.”

21.anchor: it must have no flaw.
21.note: tn Heb “all/any flaw shall not be in it.”

22.anchor: something blind, or with a broken bone, or mutilated, or with a running sore,
22.note: tn Or perhaps “a wart” (cf. NIV; HALOT 383 s.v. יַבֶּלֶת, but see the remarks in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 358).

22.anchor: or with a festering eruption, or with a feverish rash.
22.note: sn See the note on Lev 21:20 above.

22.anchor: You must not give any of these as a gift
22.note: sn This term for offering “gift” is explained in the note on Lev 1:9.

23.anchor: As for an ox
23.note: tn Heb “And an ox.”

23.anchor: or a sheep with a limb too long or stunted,
23.note: tn Heb “and stunted” (see HALOT 1102 s.v. I קלט).

23.anchor: you may present it as a freewill offering, but it will not be acceptable for a votive offering.
23.note: sn The freewill offering was voluntary, so the regulations regarding it were more relaxed. Once a vow was made, the paying of it was not voluntary (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 151-52, for very helpful remarks on this verse).

24.anchor: something with testicles that are bruised, crushed, torn, or cut off;
24.note: sn Compare Lev 21:20b.

25.anchor: Even from a foreigner
25.note: tn Heb “And from the hand of a son of a foreigner.”

25.anchor: you must not present the food of your God from such animals as these, for they are ruined and flawed;
25.note: tn Heb “for their being ruined [is] in them, flaw is in them”; NRSV “are mutilated, with a blemish in them”; NIV “are deformed and have defects.” The MT term מָשְׁחָתָם (moshkhatam, “their being ruined”) is a Muqtal form (= Hophal participle) from שָׁחַת (shakhat, “to ruin”). Smr has plural בהם משׁחתים (“deformities in them”; cf. the LXX translation). The Qumran Leviticus scroll (11QpaleoLev) has תימ הם[…], in which case the restored participle would appear to be the same as Smr, but there is no בְּ (bet) preposition before the pronoun, yielding “they are deformed” (see D. N. Freedman and K. A. Mathews, The Paleo-Hebrew Leviticus Scroll, 41 and the remarks in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 358).

27.anchor: “When an ox, lamb, or goat is born, it must be under the care of
27.note: tn The words “the care of” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied. Although many modern English versions render “with its mother” (e.g., NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT), the literal phrase “under its mother” refers to the young animal nursing from its mother. Cf. KJV, ASV “it shall be seven days under the dam,” which would probably be misunderstood.

27.anchor: its mother seven days, but from the eighth day onward it will be acceptable as an offering gift
27.note: tn Heb “for an offering of a gift.”

28.anchor: You must not slaughter an ox or a sheep and its young
28.note: tn Heb “And an ox or a sheep, it and its son, you shall not slaughter.”

28.anchor: on the same day.
28.note: tn Heb “in one day.”

29.anchor: , you must sacrifice it so that it is acceptable for your benefit.
29.note: tn Heb “for your acceptance” (see the notes on Lev 1:3-4 and 22:19 above).

30.anchor: On that very day
30.note: tn Heb “On that day”; NIV, NCV “that same day.”

30.anchor: it must be eaten; you must not leave any part of it
30.note: tn Heb “from it.”

31.anchor: “You must be sure to do my commandments.
31.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep my commandments and you shall do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 20:8, etc.).

33.anchor: the one who brought you out from the land of Egypt to be your God.
33.note: tn Heb “to be to you for God.”

2.anchor: “Command the Israelites to bring
2.note: tn Heb “and let them take.” The simple vav (ו) on the imperfect/jussive form of the verb לָקַח (laqakh, “to take”) following the imperative (“Command”) indicates a purpose clause (“to bring…”).

2.anchor: to you pure oil of beaten olives for the light, to make a lamp burn continually.
2.note: tn Heb “to cause to ascend a lamp continually.”

3.anchor: Outside the veil-canopy
3.note: tn The Hebrew term פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet) is usually translated “veil” or “curtain,” but it seems to have stretched not only in front of but also over the top of the ark of the covenant which stood behind and under it inside the most holy place (see R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 3:687-89).

3.anchor: of the congregation in the Meeting Tent Aaron
3.note: tc Several medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, and the LXX add “and his sons.”

3.anchor: continually. This is a perpetual statute throughout your generations.
3.note: tn Heb “for your generations.”

4.anchor: On the ceremonially pure lampstand
4.note: tn Alternatively, “pure [gold] lampstand,” based on Exod 25:31, etc., where the term for “gold” actually appears (see NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT, and the remarks in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 395, etc.). However, in Lev 24:4 the adjective “pure” is feminine, corresponding to “lampstand,” not an assumed noun “gold” (contrast Exod 25:31), and the “table” in v. 6 was overlaid with gold, but was not made of pure gold. Therefore, it is probably better to translate “[ceremonially] pure lampstand” (v. 4) and “[ceremonially] pure table” (v. 6); see NEB; cf. KJV, ASV; B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 164-65; and G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 307.

5.anchor: “You must take choice wheat flour
5.note: sn See the note on Lev 2:1.

5.anchor: and bake twelve loaves;
5.note: tn Heb “and bake it twelve loaves”; KJV, NAB, NASB “cakes.”

5.anchor: there must be two tenths of an ephah of flour in
5.note: tn The words “of flour” are supplied in the translation for clarity.sn See the note on Lev 5:11.

6.anchor: and you must set them in two rows, six in a row,
6.note: tn Heb “six of the row.”

7.anchor: You must put pure frankincense
7.note: tn This is not just any “incense” (קְטֹרֶת, qÿtoret; R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 3:913-16), but specifically “frankincense” (לְבֹנָה, lÿvonah; R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:756-57).

7.anchor: on each row,
7.note: tn Heb “on [עַל, ’al] the row,” probably used distributively, “on each row” (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 395-96). Perhaps the frankincense was placed “with” or “along side of” each row, not actually on the bread itself, and was actually burned as incense to the Lord (cf. NIV “Along [Alongside CEV] each row”; NRSV “with each row”; NLT “near each row”; B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 165). This particular preposition can have such a meaning.

7.anchor: and it will become a memorial portion
7.note: sn The “memorial portion” (אַזְכָרָה, ’azkharah) was normally the part of the grain offering that was burnt on the altar (see Lev 2:2 and the notes there), as opposed to the remainder, which was normally consumed by the priests (Lev 2:3; see the full regulations in Lev 6:14-23 [6:7-16 HT]).

7.anchor: for the bread, a gift
7.note: sn See the note on Lev 1:9 regarding the term “gift.”

8.anchor: Each Sabbath day
8.note: tn Heb “In the day of the Sabbath, in the day of the Sabbath.” The repetition is distributive. A few medieval Hebrew mss, the LXX, and the Syriac delete the second occurrence of the expression.

8.anchor: Aaron
8.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Aaron) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

8.anchor: continually; this portion
8.note: tn The word “portion” is supplied in the translation here for clarity, to specify what “this” refers to.

9.anchor: It will belong to Aaron and his sons, and they must eat it in a holy place because it is most holy to him, a perpetual allotted portion
9.note: tn Or “a perpetual regulation”; NRSV “a perpetual due.”

10.anchor: Now
10.note: tn Heb “And.”

10.anchor: an Israelite woman’s son whose father was an Egyptian went out among the Israelites, and the Israelite woman’s son and an Israelite man
10.note: tn Heb “the Israelite man,” but Smr has no article, and the point is that there was a conflict between the man of mixed background and a man of full Israelite descent.

11.anchor: The Israelite woman’s son misused the Name and cursed,
11.note: tn The verb rendered “misused” means literally “to bore through, to pierce” (HALOT 719 s.v. נקב qal); it is from נָקַב (naqav), not קָבַב (qavav; see the participial form in v. 16a). Its exact meaning here is uncertain. The two verbs together may form a hendiadys, “he pronounced by cursing blasphemously” (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 166), the idea being one of the following: (1) he pronounced the name “Yahweh” in a way or with words that amounted to “some sort of verbal aggression against Yahweh himself” (E. S. Gerstenberger, Leviticus [OTL], 362), (2) he pronounced a curse against the man using the name “Yahweh” (N. H. Snaith, Leviticus and Numbers [NCBC], 110; G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 311), or (3) he pronounced the name “Yahweh” and thereby blasphemed, since the “Name” was never to be pronounced (a standard Jewish explanation). In one way or another, the offense surely violated Exod 20:7, one of the ten commandments, and the same verb for cursing is used explicitly in Exod 22:28 (27 HT) prohibition against “cursing” God. For a full discussion of these and related options for interpreting this verse see P. J. Budd, Leviticus (NCBC), 335-36; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 408-9; and Levine, 166.

12.anchor: So they placed him in custody until they were able
12.note: tn The words “until they were able” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied.

tn The Hebrew here is awkward. A literal reading would be something like the following: “And they placed him in custody to give a clear decision [HALOT 976 s.v. פרשׁ qal] for themselves on the mouth of the Lord.” In any case, they were apparently waiting for a direct word from the Lord regarding this matter (see vv. 13ff).

14.anchor: “Bring the one who cursed outside the camp, and all who heard him are to lay their hands on his head, and the whole congregation is to stone him to death.
14.note: tn The words “to death” are supplied in the translation as a clarification; they are clearly implied from v. 16.

15.anchor: Moreover,
15.note: tn Heb “And.”

15.anchor: you are to tell the Israelites, ‘If any man curses his God
15.note: sn See the note on v. 11 above and esp. Exod 22:28 [27 HT].

16.anchor: and one who misuses
16.note: sn See the note on v. 11 above.

17.anchor: “‘If a man beats any person to death,
17.note: tn Heb “And if a man strikes any soul [נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh] of mankind.” The idiom seems to derive from the idea of striking a fatal blow to the very “life” (literally, “soul”) of a human being, not just landing a blow on their body (HALOT 698 s.v. נכה hif.2). On the difficult of the meaning and significance of the term נֶפֶשׁ see the notes on Lev 17:10-11.

18.anchor: One who beats an animal to death
18.note: tn Heb “And one who strikes a soul of an animal.”

18.anchor: must make restitution for it, life for life.
18.note: tn Heb “soul under soul.” Cf. KJV “beast for beast”; NCV “must give…another animal to take its place.”

19.anchor: If a man inflicts an injury on
19.note: tn Heb “gives a flaw in”; KJV, ASV “cause a blemish in.”

19.anchor: his fellow citizen,
19.note: tn Or “neighbor” (so NAB, NASB, NIV); TEV, NLT “another person.”

20.anchor: fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth – just as he inflicts an injury on another person
20.note: tn Heb “in the man [אָדָם, ’adam].”

20.anchor: that same injury
20.note: tn Heb “just as he inflicts an injury…it must be inflicted on him.” The referent (“that same injury”) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

21.anchor: One who beats an animal to death
21.note: sn See the note on v. 18 above.

21.anchor: must make restitution for it, but
21.note: tn Heb “and,” but here the Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) is adversative, contrasting the consequences of beating an animal to death with those of beating a person to death.

22.anchor: There will be one regulation
22.note: tn Heb “a regulation of one”; KJV, ASV “one manner of law”; NASB “one standard.”

2.anchor: “Speak to the Israelites and tell them, ‘When you enter the land that I am giving you, the land must observe a Sabbath
2.note: tn Heb “the land shall rest a Sabbath.”

3.anchor: Six years you may sow your field, and six years you may prune your vineyard and gather the produce,
3.note: tn Heb “its produce,” but the feminine pronoun “its” probably refers to the “land” (a feminine noun in Hebrew; cf. v. 2), not the “field” or the “vineyard,” both of which are normally masculine nouns (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 170).

4.anchor: but in the seventh year the land must have a Sabbath of complete rest
4.note: tn Heb “and in the seventh year a Sabbath of complete rest shall be to the land.” The expression “a Sabbath of complete rest” is superlative, emphasizing the full and all inclusive rest of the seventh year of the sabbatical cycle. Cf. ASV “a sabbath of solemn rest”; NAB “a complete rest.”

4.anchor: . You must not sow your field or
4.note: tn Heb “and.” Here the Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) has an alternative sense (“or”).

5.anchor: You must not gather in the aftergrowth of your harvest and you must not pick the grapes of your unpruned
5.note: tn Heb “consecrated, devoted, forbidden” (נָזִיר, nazir). The same term is used for the “consecration” of the “Nazirite” (and his hair, Num 6:2, 18, etc.), a designation which, in turn, derives from the very same root.

6.anchor: You may have the Sabbath produce
6.note: tn The word “produce” is not in the Hebrew text but is implied; cf. NASB “the sabbath products.”

6.anchor: of the land to eat – you, your male servant, your female servant, your hired worker, the resident foreigner who stays with you,
6.note: tn A “resident who stays” would be a foreign person who was probably residing as another kind of laborer in the household of a landowner (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 170-71). See v. 35 below.

7.anchor: your cattle, and the wild animals that are in your land – all its produce will be for you
7.note: tn The words “for you” are implied.

8.anchor: “‘You must count off
8.note: tn Heb “And you shall count off for yourself.”

8.anchor: seven weeks of years, seven times seven years,
8.note: tn Heb “seven years seven times.”

8.anchor: and the days of the seven weeks of years will amount to forty-nine years.
8.note: tn Heb “and they shall be for you, the days of the seven Sabbaths of years, forty-nine years.”

9.anchor: You must sound loud horn blasts
9.note: sn On the “loud horn blasts” see the note on Lev 23:24, but unlike the language there, the Hebrew term for “horn” (שׁוֹפָר, shofar) actually appears here in this verse (twice).

10.anchor: So you must consecrate the fiftieth year,
10.note: tn Heb “the year of the fifty years,” or perhaps “the year, fifty years” (GKC 435 §134.o, note 2).

10.anchor: and you must proclaim a release
10.note: tn Cf. KJV, ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV “liberty”; TEV, CEV “freedom.” The characteristics of this “release” are detailed in the following verses. For substantial summaries and bibliography on the biblical and ancient Near Eastern material regarding such a “release” see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 427-34, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 270-74.

10.anchor: in the land for all its inhabitants. That year will be your jubilee;
10.note: tn Heb “A jubilee that shall be to you.” Although there has been some significant debate about the original meaning of the Hebrew word translated “jubilee” (יוֹבֵל, yovel; see the summary in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 434), the term most likely means “ram” and can refer also to a “ram’s horn.” The fiftieth year would, therefore, be called the “jubilee” because of the associated sounding of the “ram’s horn” (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 172, and the literature cited there).

10.anchor: each one of you must return
10.note: tn Heb “you [plural] shall return, a man.”

11.anchor: That fiftieth year will be your jubilee; you must not sow the land, harvest its aftergrowth, or pick the grapes of its unpruned vines.
11.note: tn Heb “you shall not sow and you shall not…and you shall not….”sn See v. 5 above and the notes there.

12.anchor: Because that year is a jubilee, it will be holy to you – you may eat its produce
12.note: tn That is, the produce of the land (fem.; cf. v. 7 above).

13.anchor: “‘In this year of jubilee you must each return
13.note: tn Heb “you [plural] shall return, a man.”

14.anchor: If you make a sale
14.note: tn Heb “sell a sale.”

14.anchor: to your fellow citizen
14.note: tn Or “to one of your countrymen” (NIV); NASB “to your friend.”

14.anchor: or buy
14.note: tn The Hebrew infinitive absolute קָנֹה (qanoh, “buying”) substitutes for the finite verb here in sequence with the previous finite verb “sell” at the beginning of the verse (see GKC 345 §113.z).

14.anchor: from your fellow citizen, no one is to wrong his brother.
14.note: tn Heb “do not oppress a man his brother.” Here “brother” does not refer only to a sibling, but to a fellow Israelite.

15.anchor: You may buy it from your fellow citizen according to the number of years since
15.note: tn Heb “in the number of years after.”

15.anchor: the last jubilee; he may sell it to you according to the years of produce that are left.
15.note: tn The words “that are left” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied.sn The purchaser is actually buying only the crops that the land will produce until the next jubilee, since the land will revert to the original owner at that time. The purchaser, therefore, is not actually buying the land itself.

16.anchor: The more years there are,
16.note: tn Heb “To the mouth of the many years.”

16.anchor: the more you may make its purchase price, and the fewer years there are,
16.note: tn Heb “to the mouth of the few years.”

16.anchor: the less you must make its purchase price, because he is only selling to you a number of years of
16.note: tn Heb “a number of produce”; the words “years of” are implied. As an alternative this could be translated “a number of harvests” (cf. NRSV, NLT).

17.anchor: No one is to oppress his fellow citizen,
17.note: tn Heb “And you shall not oppress a man his fellow citizen.”

18.anchor: You must obey my statutes and my regulations; you must be sure to keep them
18.note: tn Heb “And you shall keep and do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 20:8, etc.).

18.anchor: so that you may live securely in the land.
18.note: tn Heb “and you shall dwell on the land to security.”

19.anchor: “‘The land will give its fruit and you may eat until you are satisfied,
19.note: tn Heb “eat to satisfaction”; KJV, ASV “ye shall eat your fill.”

21.anchor: I will command my blessing for you in the sixth year so that it may yield
21.note: tn Heb “and it [i.e., the land] shall make the produce.” The Hebrew term וְעָשָׂת (vÿ’asat, “and it shall make”) is probably an older third feminine singular form of the verb (GKC 210 §75.m). Smr has the normal form.

21.anchor: the produce
21.note: tn Smr and LXX have “its produce” (cf. 25:3, 7, etc.) rather than “the produce.”

22.anchor: and you may sow the eighth year and eat from that sixth year’s produce
22.note: tn Heb “the produce,” referring to “the produce” of the sixth year of v. 21. The words “sixth year” are supplied for clarity.

22.anchor: – old produce. Until you bring in the ninth year’s produce,
22.note: tn Heb “until the ninth year, until bringing [in] its produce.”

23.anchor: The land must not be sold without reclaim
23.note: tn The term rendered “without reclaim” means that the land has been bought for the full price and is, therefore, not subject to reclaim under any circumstances. This was not to be done with land in ancient Israel (contrast the final full sale of houses in v. 30; see the evidence cited in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 174).

23.anchor: because the land belongs to me, for you are foreigners and residents with me.
23.note: tn That is, the Israelites were strangers and residents who were attached to the Lord’s household. They did not own the land. Note the parallel to the “priest’s lodger” in Lev 22:10.

24.anchor: In all your landed property
24.note: tn Heb “And in all the land of your property.”

24.anchor: you must provide for the right of redemption of the land.
24.note: tn Heb “right of redemption you shall give to the land”; NAB “you must permit the land to be redeemed.”

25.anchor: “‘If your brother becomes impoverished and sells some of his property, his near redeemer is to come to you and redeem what his brother sold.
25.note: tn Heb “the sale of his brother.”

26.anchor: If a man has no redeemer, but he prospers
26.note: tn Heb “and his hand reaches.”

26.anchor: and gains enough for its redemption,
26.note: tn Heb “and he finds as sufficiency of its redemption.”

27.anchor: he is to calculate the value of the years it was sold,
27.note: tn Heb “and he shall calculate its years of sale.”

27.anchor: refund the balance
27.note: tn Heb “and return the excess.”

28.anchor: If he has not prospered enough to refund
28.note: tn Heb “And if his hand has not found sufficiency of returning.” Although some versions take this to mean that he has not made enough to regain the land (e.g., NASB, NRSV; see also B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 176), the combination of terms in Hebrew corresponds to the portion of v. 27 that refers specifically to refunding the money (cf. v. 27; see NIV and G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 315).

28.anchor: a balance to him, then what he sold
28.note: tn Heb “his sale.”

28.anchor: will belong to
28.note: tn Heb “will be in the hand of.” This refers to the temporary control of the one who purchased its produce until the next year of jubilee, at which time it would revert to the original owner.

28.anchor: the one who bought it until the jubilee year, but it must revert
28.note: tn Heb “it shall go out” (so KJV, ASV; see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 176).

28.anchor: in the jubilee and the original owner
28.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the original owner of the land) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

29.anchor: “‘If a man sells a residential house in a walled city,
29.note: tn Heb “a house of a residence of a walled city.”

29.anchor: its right of redemption must extend
29.note: tn Heb “shall be.”

29.anchor: until one full year from its sale;
29.note: tn Heb “of its sale.”

29.anchor: its right of redemption must extend to a full calendar year.
29.note: tn Heb “days its right of redemption shall be” (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 176).

30.anchor: If it is not redeemed before the full calendar year is ended,
30.note: tn Heb “until fulfilling to it a complete year.’

30.anchor: the house in the walled city
30.note: tn Heb “the house which [is] in the city which to it [is] a wall.” The Kethib has לֹא (lo’, “no, not”) rather than לוֹ (lo, “to it”) which is the Qere.

30.anchor: will belong without reclaim
30.note: tn See the note on v. 23 above.

31.note: tn Heb “And the houses of the villages.”

31.anchor: which have no wall surrounding them
31.note: tn Heb “which there is not to them a wall.”

31.anchor: must be considered as the field

32.anchor: the cities of the Levites, the houses in the cities which they possess,
32.note: tn Heb “the houses of the cities of their property.”

33.anchor: Whatever someone among the Levites might redeem – the sale of a house which is his property in a city – must revert in the jubilee,
33.note: tn Heb “And which he shall redeem from the Levites shall go out, sale of house and city, his property in the jubilee.” Although the end of this verse is clear, the first part is notoriously difficult. There are five main views. (1) The first clause of the verse actually attaches to the previous verse, and refers to the fact that their houses retain a perpetual right of redemption (v. 32b), “which any of the Levites may exercise” (v. 33a; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 418, 421). (2) It refers to property that one Levite sells to another Levite, which is then redeemed by still another Levite (v. 33a). In such cases, the property reverts to the original Levite owner in the jubilee year (v. 33b; G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 321). (3) It refers to houses in a city that had come to be declared as a Levitical city but had original non-Levitical owners. Once the city was declared to belong to the Levites, however, an owner could only sell his house to a Levite, and he could only redeem it back from a Levite up until the time of the first jubilee after the city was declared to be a Levitical city. In this case the first part of the verse would be translated, “Such property as may be redeemed from the Levites” (NRSV, NJPS). At the first jubilee, however, all such houses became the property of the Levites (v. 33b; P. J. Budd, Leviticus [NCBC], 353). (4) It refers to property “which is appropriated from the Levites” (not “redeemed from the Levites,” v. 33a) by those who have bought it or taken it as security for debts owed to them by Levites who had fallen on bad times. Again, such property reverts back to the original Levite owners at the jubilee (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 177). (5) It simply refers to the fact that a Levite has the option of redeeming his house (i.e., the prefix form of the verb is taken to be subjunctive, “may or might redeem”), which he had to sell because he had fallen into debt or perhaps even become destitute. Even if he never gained the resources to do so, however, it would still revert to him in the jubilee year. The present translation is intended to reflect this latter view.

34.anchor: Moreover,
34.note: tn Heb “And.”

34.anchor: the open field areas of their cities
34.note: sn This refers to the region of fields just outside and surrounding the city where cattle were kept and garden crops were grown (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 177).

35.note: tn It is not clear to whom this refers. It is probably broader than “sibling” (cf. NRSV “any of your kin”; NLT “any of your Israelite relatives”) but some English versions take it to mean “fellow Israelite” (so TEV; cf. NAB, NIV “countrymen”) and others are ambiguous (cf. CEV “any of your people”).

35.anchor: becomes impoverished and is indebted to you,
35.note: tn Heb “and his hand slips with you.”

tn The form וָחַי (vakhay, “and shall live”) looks like the adjective “living,” but the MT form is simply the same verb written as a double ayin verb (see HALOT 309 s.v. חיה qal, and GKC 218 §76.i; cf. Lev 18:5).

35.anchor: with you like a foreign resident.
35.note: tn Heb “a foreigner and resident,” which is probably to be combined (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 170-71).

36.anchor: Do not take interest or profit from him,
36.note: tn The meaning of the terms rendered “interest” and “profit” is much debated (see the summaries in P. J. Budd, Leviticus [NCBC], 354-55 and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 178). Verse 37, however, suggests that the first refers to a percentage of money and the second percentage of produce (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 421).

36.anchor: but you must fear your God and your brother must live
36.note: tn In form the Hebrew term וְחֵי (vÿkhey, “shall live”) is the construct plural noun (i.e., “the life of”), but here it is used as the finite verb (cf. v. 35 and GKC 218 §76.i).

37.anchor: You must not lend him your money at interest and you must not sell him food for profit.
37.note: tn Heb “your money” and “your food.” With regard to “interest” and “profit” see the note on v. 36 above.

38.anchor: your God who brought you out from the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan – to be your God.
38.note: tn Heb “to be to you for a God.”

39.anchor: “‘If your brother becomes impoverished with regard to you so that he sells himself to you, you must not subject him to slave service.
39.note: tn Heb “you shall not serve against him service of a slave.” A distinction is being made here between the status of slave and indentured servant.

40.anchor: He must be with you as a hired worker, as a resident foreigner;
40.note: tn See the note on Lev 25:6 above.

41.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have adversative force here.
tn Heb “may go out from you.”

41.anchor: he and his children with him, and may return to his family and to the property of his ancestors.

42.anchor: Since they are my servants whom I brought out from the land of Egypt, they must not be sold in a slave sale.
42.note: tn Or perhaps reflexive Niphal rather than passive, “they shall not sell themselves [as in] a slave sale.”

43.anchor: You must not rule over him harshly,
43.note: tn Heb “You shall not rule in him in violence”; cf. NASB “with severity”; NIV “ruthlessly.”

44.anchor: “‘As for your male and female slaves
44.note: tn Heb “And your male slave and your female slave.” Smr has these as plural terms, “slaves,” not singular.

44.anchor: who may belong to you – you may buy male and female slaves from the nations all around you.
44.note: tn Heb “ from the nations which surround you, from them you shall buy male slave and female slave.”

45.note: tn The word “slaves” is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied here.

45.anchor: from the children of the foreigners who reside with you, and from their families that are
45.note: tn Heb “family which is” (i.e., singular rather than plural).

46.anchor: You may give them as inheritance to your children after you to possess as property. You may enslave them perpetually. However, as for your brothers the Israelites, no man may rule over his brother harshly.
46.note: tn Heb “and your brothers, the sons of Israel, a man in his brother you shall not rule in him in violence.”

47.anchor: “‘If a resident foreigner who is with you prospers
47.note: tn Heb “And if the hand of a foreigner and resident with you reaches” (cf. v. 26 for this idiom).

47.anchor: and your brother becomes impoverished with regard to him so that
47.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

47.anchor: he sells himself to a resident foreigner who is with you or to a member

48.anchor: after he has sold himself he retains a right of redemption.
48.note: tn Heb “right of redemption shall be to him.”

49.anchor: or his uncle or his cousin
49.note: tn Heb “the son of his uncle.”

49.anchor: may redeem him, or anyone of the rest of his blood relatives – his family
49.note: tn Heb “or from the remainder of his flesh from his family.”
tc The LXX, followed by the Syriac, actually has “if,” which is not in the MT.

50.anchor: He must calculate with the one who bought him the number of years

from the year he sold himself to him until the jubilee year, and the cost of his sale must correspond to the number of years, according to the rate of wages a hired worker would have earned while with him.
50.note: tn Heb “as days of a hired worker he shall be with him.” For this and the following verses see the explanation in P. J. Budd, Leviticus (NCBC), 358-59.

51.anchor: If there are still many years, in keeping with them
51.note: tn Heb “to the mouth of them.”

52.anchor: but if only a few years remain
52.note: tn Heb “but if a little remains in the years.”

53.anchor: He must be with the one who bought him
53.note: tn Heb “be with him”; the referent (the one who bought him) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
tn Heb “As a hired worker year in year.”
tn Heb “He”; the referent (the one who bought him) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

54.anchor: he is not redeemed in these ways, he must go free

55.anchor: because the Israelites are my own servants;
55.note: tn Heb “because to me the sons of Israel are servants.”

2.anchor: “Speak to the Israelites and tell them, ‘When a man makes a special votive offering
2.note: tn Cf. the note on Lev 22:21. Some take this as an expression for fulfilling a vow, “to fulfill a vow” (e.g., HALOT 927-28 s.v. פלא piel and NASB; cf. NRSV “in fulfillment of a vow”) or, alternatively, “to make a vow” or “for making a vow” (HALOT 928 s.v. פלא piel [II פלא]). Perhaps it refers to the making a special vow, from the verb פָלָא (pala’, “to be wonderful; to be remarkable”), cf. Milgrom, Numbers [JPSTC], 44. B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 151 and 193, suggests that this is a special term for “setting aside a votive offering” (related to פָלָה, palah, “to set aside”). In general, the point of the expression seems to be that this sacrifice is a special gift to God that arose out of special circumstances in the life of the worshiper.

tn Heb “in your valuation, persons to the Lord,” but “in your valuation” is a frozen form and, therefore, the person (“your”) does not figure into the translation (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 73). Instead of offering a person to the Lord one could redeem that person with the appropriate amount of money delineated in the following verses (see the note on Lev 5:15 above and the explanation in Hartley, 480-81).

3.anchor: the conversion value of the male
3.note: tn Heb “your conversion value shall be [for] the male.”

3.anchor: from twenty years old up to sixty years old
3.note: tn Heb “from a son of twenty years and until a son of sixty years.”

3.anchor: is fifty shekels by the standard of the sanctuary shekel.
3.note: tn See the note on Lev 5:15.

6.anchor: If the person is one month old up to five years old, the conversion value of the male is five shekels of silver,
6.note: tn Heb “five shekels silver.”

8.anchor: If he is too poor to pay the conversion value, he must stand the person before the priest and the priest will establish his conversion value;
8.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall cause him to be valued.”

8.anchor: according to what the man who made the vow can afford,
8.note: tn Heb “on the mouth which the hand of the one who vowed reaches.”

9.anchor: “‘If what is vowed is a kind of animal from which an offering may be presented
9.note: tn Heb “which they may present from it an offering.” The plural active verb is sometimes best rendered in the passive (GKC 460 §144.f, g). Some medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, a ms of the Targum, and the Vulgate all have the singular verb instead (cf. similarly v. 11).

9.anchor: from this kind of animal
9.note: tn Heb “from it.” The masculine suffix “it” here is used for the feminine in the MT, but one medieval Hebrew ms, some mss of Smr, the LXX, and the Syriac have the feminine. The referent (this kind of animal) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

10.anchor: He must not replace or exchange it, good for bad or bad for good, and if he does indeed exchange one animal for another animal, then both the original animal
10.note: tn Heb “it and its substitute.” The referent (the original animal offered) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

12.anchor: and the priest will establish its conversion value,
12.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall cause it to be valued.” See the note on v. 8 above.

13.anchor: If, however, the person who made the vow redeems the animal,
13.note: tn Heb “And if redeeming [infinitive absolute] he redeems it [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p. The referent of “he” (the person who made the vow) and “it” (the animal) have both been specified in the translation for clarity.

13.anchor: he must add one fifth to
13.note: tn Heb “on,” meaning “on top of, in addition to” (likewise in v. 15).

14.anchor: , the priest will establish its conversion value, whether good or bad. Just as the priest establishes its conversion value, thus it will stand.
14.note: tn The expression “it shall stand” may be a technical term for “it shall be legally valid”; cf. NLT “assessment will be final.”

15.anchor: If the one who consecrates it redeems his house, he must add to it one fifth of its conversion value in silver, and it will belong to him.
15.note: tn Heb “and it shall be to him.”

16.anchor: some of his own landed property, the conversion value must be calculated in accordance with the amount of seed needed to sow it,
16.note: tn Heb “a conversion value shall be to the mouth of its seed.”

16.anchor: a homer of barley seed being priced at fifty shekels of silver.
16.note: tn Heb “seed of a homer of barley in fifty shekels of silver.”

17.anchor: If he consecrates his field in the jubilee year,
17.note: tn Heb “from the year of the jubilee.” For the meaning of “jubilee,” see the note on Lev 25:10 above.

18.anchor: but if
18.note: tn Heb “And if.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have adversative force here.

18.anchor: he consecrates his field after the jubilee, the priest will calculate the price
18.note: tn Heb “the silver.”

19.anchor: If, however, the one who consecrated the field redeems it,
19.note: tn Heb “And if redeeming [infinitive absolute] he redeems [finite verb] the field, the one who consecrated it.” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

19.anchor: he must add to it one fifth of the conversion price
19.note: tn Heb “the silver of the conversion value.”

19.anchor: and it will belong to him.
19.note: tn Heb “and it shall rise to him.” See HALOT 1087 s.v. קום 7 for the rendering offered here, but see also the note on the end of v. 14 above (cf. J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 476, 478).

20.anchor: If he does not redeem the field, but sells
20.note: tn Heb “and if he sells.”

21.anchor: When it reverts
21.note: tn Heb “When it goes out” (cf. Lev 25:25-34).

21.anchor: like a permanently dedicated field;
21.note: tn Heb “like the field of the permanent dedication.” The Hebrew word חֵרֶם (kherem) is a much discussed term. In this and the following verses it refers in a general way to the fact that something is permanently devoted to the Lord and therefore cannot be redeemed (cf. v. 20b). See J. A. Naudé, NIDOTTE 2:276-77; N. Lohfink, TDOT 5:180-99, esp. pp. 184, 188, and 198-99; and the numerous explanations in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 483-85.

21.anchor: it will become the priest’s property.
21.note: tn Heb “to the priest it shall be his property.”

22.anchor: a field he has purchased,
22.note: tn Heb “his field of purchase,” which is to be distinguished from his own ancestral “landed property” (cf. v. 16 above).

23.anchor: the priest will calculate for him the amount of its conversion value until the jubilee year, and he must pay
23.note: tn Heb “give” (so KJV, ASV, NASB, NLT).

25.anchor: Every conversion value must be calculated by the standard of the sanctuary shekel;
25.note: tn See the note on Lev 5:15.

26.note: tn Heb “to the Lord it is.”

27.anchor: If, however,
27.note: tn Heb “And if.”

27.anchor: it is among the unclean animals, he may ransom it according to
27.note: tn Heb “in” or “by.”

28.anchor: Lord
28.note: tn Heb “Surely, any permanently dedicated [thing] which a man shall permanently dedicate to the Lord.” The Hebrew term חֵרֶם (kherem) refers to things that are devoted permanently to the Lord (see the note on v. 21 above).

29.anchor: Any human being who is permanently dedicated
29.note: tn Heb “permanently dedicated from among men.”

30.anchor: “‘Any tithe
30.note: tn On the “tithe” system in Israel, see R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:1035-55 and esp. pp. 1041-42 on Lev 27:30-33.

31.note: tn Heb “And if redeeming [infinitive absolute] a man redeems [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

31.anchor: part of his tithe, however, he must add one fifth to it.
31.note: tn Heb “its one fifth on it.”

32.note: sn The tithed animal was the tenth one that passed under the shepherd’s rod or staff as they were being counted (see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 485, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 200).

33.anchor: The owner
33.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the owner of the animal) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

33.anchor: must not examine the animals to distinguish between good and bad, and he must not exchange it. If, however, he does exchange it,
33.note: tn Heb “And if exchanging [infinitive absolute] he exchanges it [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

33.anchor: both the original animal
33.note: tn Heb “it and its substitute.” The referent (the original animal offered) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

33.anchor: and its substitute will be holy.
33.note: tn Heb “it shall be and its substitute shall be holy.”

34.anchor: commanded Moses to tell the Israelites
34.note: tn Most of the commentaries and English versions translate, “which the Lord commanded Moses for the children of Israel.” The preposition אֶל (’el), however, does not usually mean “for.” In this book it is commonly used when the Lord commands Moses “to speak [un]to” a person or group of persons (see, e.g., Lev 1:2; 4:2, etc.). The translation “to tell” here reflects this pattern in the book of Leviticus.

1.anchor: “‘You must not make for yourselves idols,
1.note: sn For the literature regarding the difficult etymology and meaning of the term for “idols” (אֱלִילִם, ’elilim), see the literature cited in the note on Lev 19:4. It appears to be a diminutive play on words with אֵל (’el, “god, God”) and, perhaps at the same time, recalls a common Semitic word for “worthless, weak, powerless, nothingness.” Snaith suggests a rendering of “worthless godlings.”

1.anchor: so you must not set up for yourselves a carved image or a pillar, and you must not place a sculpted stone in your land to bow down before
1.note: tn Heb “on.” The “sculpted stone” appears to be some sort of stone with images carved into (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 181, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 449).

2.anchor: You must keep my Sabbaths and reverence
2.note: tn Heb “and my sanctuary you shall fear.” Cf. NCV “respect”; CEV “honor.”

3.anchor: “‘If you walk in my statutes and are sure to obey my commandments,
3.note: tn Heb “and my commandments you shall keep and do them.” This appears to be a kind of verbal hendiadys, where the first verb is a modifier of the action of the second verb (see GKC 386 §120.d, although שָׁמַר [shamar, “to keep”] is not cited there; cf. Lev 20:8; 25:18, etc.).

4.anchor: I will give you your rains in their time so that
4.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

4.anchor: the land will give its yield and the trees of the field will produce their fruit.
4.note: tn Heb “the tree of the field will give its fruit.” As a collective singular this has been translated as plural.

5.anchor: Threshing season will extend for you until the season for harvesting grapes,
5.note: tn Heb “will reach for you the vintage season.”

5.anchor: and the season for harvesting grapes will extend until sowing season, so
5.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

5.anchor: you will eat your bread until you are satisfied,
5.note: tn Heb “to satisfaction”; KJV, ASV, NASB “to the full.”

6.anchor: I will grant peace in the land so that
6.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have resultative force here.

6.anchor: you will lie down to sleep without anyone terrifying you.
6.note: tn Heb “and there will be no one who terrifies.” The words “to sleep” have been supplied in the translation for clarity.

6.anchor: I will remove harmful animals
6.note: tn Heb “harmful animal,” singular, but taken here as a collective plural (so almost all English versions).

6.anchor: from the land, and no sword of war
6.note: tn Heb “no sword”; the words “of war” are supplied in the translation to indicate what the metaphor of the sword represents.

7.anchor: You will pursue your enemies and they will fall before you by the sword.
7.note: tn Heb “to the sword.”

9.anchor: I will turn to you, make you fruitful, multiply you, and maintain
9.note: tn Heb “cause to arise,” but probably used here for the Lord’s intention of confirming or maintaining the covenant commitment made at Sinai. Cf. KJV “establish”; NASB “will confirm”; NAB “carry out”; NIV “will keep.”

10.anchor: You will still be eating stored produce from the previous year
10.note: tn Heb “old [produce] growing old.”

10.anchor: and will have to clean out what is stored from the previous year to make room for new.
10.note: tn Heb “and old from the presence of new you will bring out.”

11.anchor: “‘I will put my tabernacle
11.note: tn LXX codexes Vaticanus and Alexandrinus have “my covenant” rather than “my tabernacle.” Cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV “my dwelling.”

11.anchor: in your midst and I will not abhor you.
11.note: tn Heb “and my soul [נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh] will not abhor you.”

13.anchor: your God who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from being their slaves,
13.note: tn Heb “from being to them slaves.”

13.anchor: and I broke the bars of your yoke and caused you to walk upright.
13.note: tn In other words, to walk as free people and not as slaves. Cf. NIV “with (+ your CEV, NLT) heads held high”; NCV “proudly.”

14.anchor: “‘If, however,
14.note: tn Heb “And if.”

14.anchor: you do not obey me and keep
14.note: tn Heb “and do not do.”

15.anchor: if you reject my statutes and abhor my regulations so that you do not keep
15.note: tn Heb “to not do.”

16.anchor: I for my part
16.note: tn Or “I also” (see HALOT 76 s.v. אַף 6.b).

16.anchor: will do this to you: I will inflict horror on you, consumption and fever, which diminish eyesight and drain away the vitality of life.
16.note: tn Heb “soul.” These expressions may refer either to the physical effects of consumption and fever as the rendering in the text suggests (e.g., J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 452, 454, “diminishing eyesight and loss of appetite”), or perhaps the more psychological effects, “which exhausts the eyes” because of anxious hope “and causes depression” (Heb “causes soul [נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh] to pine away”), e.g., B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 185.

16.anchor: You will sow your seed in vain because
16.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have causal force here.

16.anchor: your enemies will eat it.
16.note: tn That is, “your enemies will eat” the produce that grows from the sown seed.

18.anchor: “‘If, in spite of all these things,
18.note: tn Heb “And if until these.”

18.anchor: you do not obey me, I will discipline you seven times more on account of your sins.
18.note: tn Heb “I will add to discipline you seven [times] on your sins.”

20.anchor: Your strength will be used up in vain, your land will not give its yield, and the trees of the land
20.note: tn Heb “the tree of the land will not give its fruit.” The collective singular has been translated as a plural. Tg. Onq., some medieval Hebrew mss, Smr, LXX, and Tg. Ps.-J. have “the field” as in v. 4, rather than “the land.”

21.anchor: “‘If you walk in hostility against me
21.note: tn Heb “hostile with me,” but see the added preposition בְּ (bet) on the phrase “in hostility” in v. 24 and 27.

21.anchor: and are not willing to obey me, I will increase your affliction
21.note: tn Heb “your blow, stroke”; cf. TEV “punishment”; NLT “I will inflict you with seven more disasters.”

22.anchor: I will send the wild animals
22.note: tn Heb “the animal of the field.” This collective singular has been translated as a plural. The expression “animal of the field” refers to a wild (i.e., nondomesticated) animal.

22.anchor: against you and they will bereave you of your children,
22.note: tn The words “of your children” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied.

22.anchor: annihilate your cattle, and diminish your population
22.note: tn Heb “and diminish you.”

23.anchor: “‘If in spite of these things
23.note: tn Heb “And if in these.”

23.anchor: you do not allow yourselves to be disciplined and you walk in hostility against me,
23.note: tn Heb “with me,” but see the added preposition בְּ (bet) on the phrase “in hostility” in vv. 24 and 27.

24.anchor: I myself will also walk in hostility against you and strike you
24.note: tn Heb “and I myself will also strike you.”

25.anchor: I will bring on you an avenging sword, a covenant vengeance.
25.note: tn Heb “vengeance of covenant”; cf. NAB “the avenger of my covenant.”

25.anchor: Although
25.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) has a concessive force in this context.

25.anchor: you will gather together into your cities, I will send pestilence among you and you will be given into enemy hands.
25.note: tn Heb “in hand of enemy,” but Tg. Ps.-J. and Tg. Neof. have “in the hands of your enemies” (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 454).

26.anchor: When I break off your supply of bread,
26.note: tn Heb “When I break to you staff of bread” (KJV, ASV, and NASB all similar).

26.anchor: ten women will bake your bread in one oven; they will ration your bread by weight,
26.note: tn Heb “they will return your bread in weight.”

27.anchor: “‘If in spite of this
27.note: tn Heb “And if in this.”

27.anchor: you do not obey me but walk in hostility against me,
27.note: tn Heb “with me.”

28.anchor: I will walk in hostile rage against you
28.note: tn Heb “in rage of hostility with you”; NASB “with wrathful hostility”; NRSV “I will continue hostile to you in fury”; CEV “I’ll get really furious.”

29.anchor: You will eat the flesh of your sons and the flesh of your daughters.
29.note: tn Heb “and the flesh of your daughters you will eat.” The phrase “you will eat” has not been repeated in the translation for stylistic reasons.

30.anchor: I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars,
30.note: sn Regarding these cultic installations, see the remarks in B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 188, and R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:903. The term rendered “incense altars” might better be rendered “sanctuaries [of foreign deities]” or “stelae.”

30.anchor: and I will stack your dead bodies on top of the lifeless bodies of your idols.
30.note: tn The translation reflects the Hebrew wordplay “your corpses…the corpses of your idols.” Since idols, being lifeless, do not really have “corpses,” the translation uses “dead bodies” for people and “lifeless bodies” for the idols.

30.anchor: I will abhor you.
30.note: tn Heb “and my soul will abhor you.”

31.anchor: I will lay your cities waste
31.note: tn Heb “And I will give your cities a waste”; NLT “make your cities desolate.”

33.anchor: I will scatter you among the nations and unsheathe the sword
33.note: tn Heb “and I will empty sword” (see HALOT 1228 s.v. ריק 3).

34.anchor: “‘Then the land will make up for
34.note: tn There are two Hebrew roots רָצָה (ratsah), one meaning “to be pleased with; to take pleasure” (HALOT 1280-81 s.v. רצה; cf. “enjoy” in NASB, NIV, NRSV, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 452), and the other meaning “to restore” (HALOT 1281-82 s.v. II רצה; cf. NAB “retrieve” and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 189).

35.anchor: All the days of the desolation it will have the rest it did not have
35.note: tn Heb “it shall rest which it did not rest.”

37.anchor: They will stumble over each other as those who flee before a sword, though
37.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) is used in a concessive sense here.

37.anchor: there is no pursuer, and there will be no one to take a stand
37.note: tn The term rendered “to stand up” is a noun, not an infinitive. It occurs only here and appears to designate someone who would take a powerful stand for them against their enemies.

39.anchor: “‘As for the ones who remain among you, they will rot away because of
39.note: tn Heb “in” (so KJV, ASV; also later in this verse).

39.anchor: their iniquity in the lands of your enemies, and they will also rot away because of their ancestors’
39.note: tn Heb “fathers’” (also in the following verse).

40.note: tn Heb “And.” Many English versions take this to be a conditional clause (“if…”) though there is no conditional particle (see, e.g., NASB, NIV, NRSV; but see the very different rendering in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 190). The temporal translation offered here (“when”) takes into account the particle אָז (’az, “then”), which occurs twice in v. 41. The obvious contextual contrast between vv. 39 and 40 is expressed by “however” in the translation.

40.anchor: they confess their iniquity and their ancestors’ iniquity which they committed by trespassing against me,
40.note: tn Heb “in their trespassing which they trespassed in me.” See the note on Lev 5:15, although the term is used in a more technical sense there in relation to the “guilt offering.”
tn Heb “and also which they walked.”

41.anchor: (and I myself will walk in hostility against them and bring them into the land of their enemies), and
41.note: tn Heb “or then,” although the LXX has “then” and the Syriac “and then.”

41.anchor: then their uncircumcised hearts become humbled and they make up for
41.note: tn Heb “and then they make up for.” On the verb “make up for” see the note on v. 34 above.

42.anchor: I will remember my covenant with Jacob and also my covenant with Isaac and also my covenant with Abraham,
42.note: tn Heb “my covenant with Abraham I will remember.” The phrase “I will remember” has not been repeated in the translation for stylistic reasons.

43.anchor: The land will be abandoned by them
43.note: tn Heb “from them.” The preposition “from” refers here to the agent of the action (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 455).

43.anchor: in order that it may make up for
43.note: tn The jussive form of the verb with the simple vav (ו) here calls for a translation that expresses purpose.

43.anchor: its Sabbaths while it is made desolate
43.note: tn The verb is the Hophal infinitive construct with the third feminine singular suffix (GKC 182 §67.y; cf. v. 34).

43.anchor: and they will make up for their iniquity because
43.note: tn Heb “because and in because,” a double expression, which is used only here and in Ezek 13:10 (without the vav) for emphasis (GKC 492 §158.b).

43.anchor: they have rejected my regulations and have abhorred
43.note: tn Heb “and their soul has abhorred.”

45.anchor: I will remember for them the covenant with their ancestors
45.note: tn Heb “covenant of former ones.” sn For similar expressions referring back to the ancestors who refused to follow the stipulations of the Mosaic covenant see, for example, Deut 19:14, Jer 11:10, and Ps 79:8 (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 192, and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 471).

46.note: tn Heb “gave” (so NLT); KJV, ASV, NCV “made.”

46.anchor: between himself and the Israelites at Mount Sinai through
46.note: tn Heb “by the hand of” (so KJV).

1.anchor: Then
1.note: tn Although it has been used elsewhere in this translation as an English variation from the ubiquitous use of vav in Hebrew, in this instance “then” as a rendering for vav is intended to show that the Nadab and Abihu catastrophe took place on the inauguration day described in Lev 9. The tragic incident in Lev 10 happened in close temporal connection to the Lord’s fire that consumed the offerings at the end of Lev 9. Thus, for example, the “sin offering” male goat referred to in Lev 10:16-19 is the very one referred to in Lev 9:15.

1.anchor: Aaron’s sons, Nadab and Abihu, each took his fire pan and put fire in it, set incense on it, and presented strange fire
1.note: tn The expression “strange fire” (אֵשׁ זָרָה, ’esh zarah) seems imprecise (cf. NAB “profane fire”; NIV “unauthorized fire”; NRSV “unholy fire”; NLT “a different kind of fire”) and has been interpreted numerous ways (see the helpful summary in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 132-33). The infraction may have involved any of the following or a combination thereof: (1) using coals from someplace other than the burnt offering altar (i.e., “unauthorized coals” according to J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:598; cf. Lev 16:12 and cf. “unauthorized person” אִישׁ זָר (’ish zar) in Num 16:40 [17:5 HT], NASB “layman”), (2) using the wrong kind of incense (cf. the Exod 30:9 regulation against “strange incense” קְטֹרֶת זָרָה (qÿtoreh zarah) on the incense altar and the possible connection to Exod 30:34-38), (3) performing an incense offering at an unprescribed time (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 59), or (4) entering the Holy of Holies at an inappropriate time (Lev 16:1-2).

2.anchor: Lord
2.note: tn See the note on 9:24a.

3.anchor: spoke: ‘Among the ones close to me I will show myself holy,
3.note: tn The Niphal verb of the Hebrew root קָדַשׁ (qadash) can mean either “to be treated as holy” (so here, e.g., BDB 873 s.v. קָּדַשׁ, LXX, NASB, and NEB) or “to show oneself holy” (so here, e.g., HALOT 1073 s.v. קדשׁnif.1, NIV, NRSV, NLT; J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:595, 601-3; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 133-34). The latter rendering seems more likely here since, in the immediate context, the Lord himself had indeed shown himself to be holy by the way he responded to the illegitimate incense offering of Nadab and Abihu. They had not treated the Lord as holy, so the Lord acted on his own behalf to show that he was indeed holy.

3.anchor: and in the presence of all the people I will be honored.’”
3.note: tn In this context the Niphal of the Hebrew root כָּבֵד (kaved) can mean “to be honored” (e.g., NASB and NIV here), “be glorified” (ASV, NRSV and NLT here), or “glorify oneself, show one’s glory” (cf. NAB; e.g., specifically in this verse HALOT 455 s.v. כבדnif.3; J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:595, 603-4; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 126, 134). Comparing this clause with the previous one (see the note above), the point may be that when the Lord shows himself to be holy as he has done in 10:1-2, this results in him being honored (i.e., reverenced, feared, treated with respect) among the people. This suggests the passive rendering. It is possible, however, that one should use the reflexive rendering here as in the previous clause. If so, the passage means that the Lord showed both his holiness and his glory in one outbreak against Nadab and Abihu.

6.anchor: Then Moses said to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his other two sons, “Do not
6.note: tc Smr has “you must not” (לֹא, lo’) rather than the MT’s “do not” (אַל, ’al; cf. the following negative לֹא, lo’, in the MT).

6.anchor: dishevel the hair of your heads
6.note: tn Heb “do not let free your heads.” Some have taken this to mean, “do not take off your headgear” (cf. NAB, NASB), but it probably also involves leaving one’s hair unkempt as a sign of mourning (see J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:608-9; cf. NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).

6.anchor: has caused,
6.note: tn Heb “shall weep [for] the burning which the Lord has burned”; NIV “may mourn for those the Lord has destroyed by fire.”

9.anchor: “Do not drink wine or strong drink, you and your sons with you, when you enter into the Meeting Tent, so that you do not die, which is a perpetual statute throughout your generations,
9.note: tn Heb “a perpetual statute for your generations”; NAB “a perpetual ordinance”; NRSV “a statute forever”; NLT “a permanent law.” The Hebrew grammar here suggests that the last portion of v. 9 functions as both a conclusion to v. 9 and an introduction to vv. 10-11. It is a pivot clause, as it were. Thus, it was a “perpetual statute” to not drink alcoholic beverages when ministering in the tabernacle, but it was also a “perpetual statue” to distinguish between holy and profane and unclean and clean (v. 10) as well as to teach the children of Israel all such statutes (v. 11).

10.anchor: as well as
10.note: tn Heb “and,” but regarding the translation “as well as,” see the note at the end of v. 9.

10.anchor: to distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean,
10.note: sn The two pairs of categories in this verse refer to: (1) the status of a person, place, thing, or time – “holy” (קֹדֶשׁ, qodesh) versus “common” (חֹל, khol); as opposed to (2) the condition of a person, place, or thing – “unclean” (טָמֵא, tame’) versus “clean” (טָהוֹר, tahor). Someone or something could gain “holy” status by being “consecrated” (i.e., made holy; e.g., the Hebrew Piel קִדֵּשׁ (qiddesh) in Lev 8:15, 30), and to treat someone or something that was holy as if it were “common” would be to “profane” that person or thing (the Hebrew Piel הִלֵּל [hillel], e.g., in Lev 19:29 and 22:15). Similarly, on another level, someone or something could be in a “clean” condition, but one could “defile” (the Hebrew Piel טִמֵּא [timme’], e.g., in Gen 34:5 and Num 6:9) that person or thing and thereby make it “unclean.” To “purify” (the Hebrew Piel טִהֵר [tiher], e.g., in Lev 16:19 and Num 8:6, 15) that unclean person or thing would be to make it “clean” once again. With regard to the animals (Lev 11), some were by nature “unclean,” so they could never be eaten, but others were by nature “clean” and, therefore, edible (Lev 11:2, 46-47). The meat of clean animals could become inedible by too long of a delay in eating it, in which case the Hebrew term פִּגּוּל (pigul) “foul, spoiled” is used to describe it (Lev 7:18; 19:7; cf. also Ezek 4:14 and Isa 65:4), not the term for “unclean” (טָהוֹר, tahor). Strictly speaking, therefore, unclean meat never becomes clean, and clean meat never becomes unclean.

11.anchor: has spoken to them through
11.note: tn Heb “by the hand of” (so KJV).

13.anchor: You must eat it in a holy place because it is your allotted portion
13.note: tn Heb “statute” (cf. 10:9, 11); cf. KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV “due”; NIV “share”; NLT “regular share.”

13.anchor: and the allotted portion of your sons from the gifts
13.note: tn For the rendering of the Hebrew אִשֶׁה (’isheh) as “gift” rather than “offering [made] by fire,” see the note on Lev 1:9.

13.anchor: , for this is what I have been commanded.
13.note: sn Cf. Lev 2:3 and 6:14-18 [6:7-11 HT] for these regulations.

14.anchor: Also, the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution offering you must eat in a ceremonially
14.note: tn The word “ceremonially” has been supplied in the translation to clarify that the cleanness of the place specified is ritual or ceremonial in nature.

14.anchor: clean place, you and your sons and daughters with you, for they have been given as your allotted portion and the allotted portion of your sons from the peace offering sacrifices of the Israelites.
14.note: sn Cf. Lev 7:14, 28-34 for these regulations.

16.anchor: Later Moses sought diligently for the sin offering male goat,
16.note: sn This is the very same male goat offered in Lev 9:15 (cf. the note on Lev 10:1 above).

16.anchor: but it had actually been burnt.
16.note: tn Heb “but behold, it had been burnt” (KJV and NASB both similar).

17.anchor: “Why did you not eat the sin offering in the sanctuary? For it is most holy and he gave it to you to bear the iniquity of the congregation,
17.note: sn This translation is quite literal. On the surface it appears to mean that the priests would “bear the iniquity” of the congregation by the act of eating the sin offering (so J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:622-25, 635-40). Such a notion is, however, found nowhere else in the Levitical regulations and seems unlikely (so J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 136). A more likely interpretation is reflected in this interpretive rendering: “he gave it to you [as payment] for [your work of] bearing the iniquity of the congregation.” The previous section of the chapter deals with the prebends that the priests received for performing the ministry of the tabernacle (Lev 10:12-15). Lev 10:16-18, therefore, seems to continue the very same topic in the light of the most immediate situation (see R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:702-4).

18.anchor: See here!
18.note: tn Or “Behold!” (so KJV, ASV, NASB).

18.anchor: Its blood was not brought into the holy place within!
18.note: sn The term here rendered “within” refers to the bringing of the blood inside the holy place for application to the altar of incense rather than to the altar of burnt offering in the courtyard of the tabernacle (cf. Lev 4:7, 16-18; 6:30 [23 HT]).

19.anchor: But Aaron spoke to Moses, “See here!
19.note: tn Or “Behold!” (so KJV, ASV, NASB); NRSV “See.”

19.anchor: have been pleased?”
19.note: tn Heb “today they presented their sin offering and their burnt offering before the Lord, and like these things have happened to me, and (if) I had eaten sin offering today would it be good in the eyes of the Lord?” The idiom “would it be good in the eyes of [the Lord]” has been translated “would [the Lord] have been pleased.” Cf. NRSV “would it have been agreeable to the Lord?”; CEV, NLT “Would the Lord have approved?”

20.anchor: When Moses heard this explanation, he was satisfied.
20.note: tn Heb “it was good in his eyes” (an idiom). Cf. KJV “he was content”; NLT “he approved.”

2.anchor: “Tell the Israelites: ‘This is the kind of creature you may eat from among all the animals
2.note: tn Heb “the animal,” but as a collective plural, and so throughout this chapter.

3.anchor: You may eat any among the animals that has a divided hoof (the hooves are completely split in two
3.note: tn Heb “every divider of hoof and cleaver of the cleft of hooves”; KJV, ASV “parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted.”

3.anchor: ) and that also chews the cud.
3.note: tn Heb “bringer up of the cud” (a few of the ancient versions include the conjunction “and,” but it does not appear in the MT). The following verses make it clear that both dividing the hoof and chewing the cud were required; one of these conditions would not be enough to make the animal suitable for eating without the other.

4.anchor: However, you must not eat these
4.note: tn Heb “this,” but as a collective plural (see the following context).

4.anchor: from among those that chew the cud and have divided hooves: The camel is unclean to you
4.note: sn Regarding “clean” versus “unclean,” see the note on Lev 10:10.

4.anchor: because it chews the cud
4.note: tn Heb “because a chewer of the cud it is” (see also vv. 5 and 6).

4.anchor: even though its hoof is not divided.
4.note: tn Heb “and hoof there is not dividing” (see also vv. 5 and 6).

5.anchor: The rock badger
5.note: sn A small animal generally understood to be Hyrax syriacus; KJV, ASV, NIV “coney”; NKJV “rock hyrax.”

7.anchor: The pig is unclean to you because its hoof is divided (the hoof is completely split in two
7.note: tn See the note on Lev 11:3.

7.anchor: ), even though it does not chew the cud.
7.note: tn The meaning and basic rendering of this clause is quite certain, but the verb for “chewing” the cud here is not the same as the preceding verses, where the expression is “to bring up the cud” (see the note on v. 3 above). It appears to be a cognate verb for the noun “cud” (גֵּרָה, gerah) and could mean either “to drag up” (i.e., from the Hebrew Qal of גָרָר [garar] meaning “to drag,” referring to the dragging the cud up and down between the stomach and mouth of the ruminant animal; so J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:647, 653) or “to chew” (i.e., from the Hebrew Niphal [or Qal B] of גָרָר used in a reciprocal sense; so J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 149, and compare BDB 176 s.v. גָרַר, “to chew,” with HALOT 204 s.v. גרר qal.B, “to ruminate”).

8.anchor: You must not eat from their meat and you must not touch their carcasses;
8.note: sn The regulations against touching the carcasses of dead unclean animals (contrast the restriction against eating their flesh) is treated in more detail in Lev 11:24-28 (cf. also vv. 29-40). For the time being, this chapter continues to develop the issue of what can and cannot be eaten.

9.anchor: “‘These you can eat from all creatures that are in the water: Any creatures in the water that have both fins and scales,
9.note: tn Heb “all which have fin and scale” (see also vv. 10 and 12).

9.anchor: whether in the seas or in the streams,
9.note: tn Heb “in the water, in the seas and in the streams” (see also vv. 10 and 12).

13.anchor: “‘These you are to detest from among the birds – they must not be eaten, because they are detestable:
13.note: tn For zoological remarks on the following list of birds see J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:662-64; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 159-60.

14.anchor: the kite, the buzzard of any kind,
14.note: tn Heb “and the buzzard to its kind” (see also vv. 16 and 19 for the same expression “of any kind”).

15.anchor: every kind of crow,
15.note: tn Heb “every crow to its kind.” Many English versions (e.g., KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) render this as “raven.”

16.anchor: the eagle owl,
16.note: tn Literally, “the daughter of the wasteland.” Various proposals for the species of bird referred to here include “owl” (KJV), “horned owl” (NIV, NCV), and “ostrich” (ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT).

20.anchor: “‘Every winged swarming thing that walks on all fours
20.note: tn Heb “the one walking on four” (cf. vv. 21-23 and 27-28).

21.anchor: However, this you may eat from all the winged swarming things that walk on all fours, which have jointed legs
21.note: tn Heb “which to it are lower legs from above to its feet” (reading the Qere “to it” rather than the Kethib “not”).

22.anchor: These you may eat from them:
22.note: tn For entomological remarks on the following list of insects see J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:665-66; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 160-61.

24.anchor: “‘By these
24.note: tn Heb “and to these.”

26.anchor: “‘All
26.note: tn Heb “to all” (cf. the note on v. 24). This and the following verses develop more fully the categories of uncleanness set forth in principle in vv. 24-25.

26.anchor: animals that divide the hoof but it is not completely split in two
26.note: tn Heb “divides hoof and cleft it does not cleave”; KJV “divideth the hoof, and is not clovenfooted”; NLT “divided but unsplit hooves.”

26.anchor: and do not chew the cud
26.note: tn See the note on Lev 11:3.

26.anchor: are unclean to you; anyone who touches them becomes unclean.
26.note: sn Compare the regulations in Lev 11:2-8.

27.anchor: All that walk on their paws among all the creatures that walk on all fours
27.note: tn Heb “the one walking on four.” Compare Lev 11:20-23.

29.anchor: “‘Now this is what is unclean to you among the swarming things that swarm on the land:
29.note: tn For zoological analyses of the list of creatures in vv. 29-30, see J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:671-72; and J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 161-62.

32.note: tn Heb “And all which it shall fall on it from them.”

32.anchor: when they die will become unclean – any wood vessel or garment or article of leather or sackcloth. Any such vessel with which work is done must be immersed in water
32.note: tn Heb “in water it shall be brought.”

33.anchor: As for any clay vessel they fall into,
33.note: tn Heb “And any earthenware vessel which shall fall from them into its midst.”

33.anchor: everything in it
33.note: tn Heb “all which is in its midst.”

34.anchor: Any food that may be eaten which becomes soaked with water
34.note: tn Heb “which water comes on it.”

34.anchor: will become unclean. Anything drinkable
34.note: tn Heb “any drink which may be drunk”; NASB “any liquid which may be drunk”; NLT “any beverage that is in such an unclean container.”

34.anchor: in any such vessel will become unclean.
34.note: tn This half of the verse assumes that the unclean carcass has fallen into the food or drink (cf. v. 33 and also vv. 35-38).

35.anchor: Anything their carcass may fall on will become unclean. An oven or small stove must be smashed to pieces; they are unclean, and they will stay unclean

36.anchor: However, a spring or a cistern which collects water
36.note: tn Heb “a spring and a cistern collection of water”; NAB, NIV “for collecting water.”

37.anchor: Now, if such a carcass falls on any sowing seed which is to be sown,
37.note: tn Heb “And if there falls from their carcass on any seed of sowing which shall be sown.”

39.note: tn This word for “animal” refers to land animal quadrupeds, not just any beast that dwells on the land (cf. 11:2).
tn Heb “which is food for you” or “which is for you to eat.”

42.anchor: You must not eat anything that crawls
42.note: tn Heb “goes” (KJV, ASV “goeth”); NIV “moves about”; NLT “slither along.” The same Hebrew term is translated “walks” in the following clause.

42.anchor: on its belly or anything that walks on all fours or on any number of legs
42.note: tn Heb “until all multiplying of legs.”

43.anchor: Do not make yourselves detestable by any of the swarming things.
43.note: tn Heb “by any of the swarming things that swarm.”

45.anchor: who brought you up from the land of Egypt to be your God,
45.note: tn Heb “to be to you for a God.”

46.note: sn The Hebrew term translated “law” (תוֹרָה, torah) introduces here a summary or colophon for all of Lev 11. Similar summaries are found in Lev 7:37-38; 13:59; 14:54-57; and 15:32-33.

46.anchor: of the land animals, the birds, all the living creatures that move in the water, and all the creatures

2.anchor: ’s appointed times which you must proclaim as holy assemblies – my appointed times:
2.note: tn Heb “these are them, my appointed times.” sn The term מוֹעֵד (mo’ed, rendered “appointed time” here) can refer to either a time or place of meeting. See the note on “tent of meeting” (אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד, ’ohel mo’ed) in Lev 1:1.

3.anchor: “‘Six days work may be done, but on the seventh day there must be a Sabbath of complete rest,
3.note: tn This is a superlative expression, emphasizing the full and all inclusive rest of the Sabbath and certain festival times throughout the chapter (see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 155). Cf. ASV “a sabbath of solemn rest.”

5.anchor: In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, at twilight,
5.note: tn Heb “between the two evenings,” perhaps designating the time between the setting of the sun and the true darkness of night. Cf. KJV, ASV “at even”; NAB “at the evening twilight.”sn See B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 156, for a full discussion of the issues raised in this verse. The rabbinic tradition places the slaughter of Passover offerings between approximately 3:00 p.m. and 6:00 p.m., not precisely at twilight. Moreover, the term פֶּסַח (pesakh) may mean “protective offering” rather than “Passover offering,” although they amount to about the same thing in the historical context of the exodus from Egypt (see Exod 11-12).

6.anchor: Then on the fifteenth day of the same month
6.note: tn Heb “to this month.”

7.anchor: On the first day there will be a holy assembly for you; you must not do any regular work.
7.note: tn Heb “work of service”; KJV “servile work”; NASB “laborious work”; TEV “daily work.”

10.anchor: “Speak to the Israelites and tell them, ‘When you enter the land that I am about to give to you and you gather in its harvest,
10.note: tn Heb “and you harvest its harvest.”

10.anchor: then you must bring the sheaf of the first portion of your harvest
10.note: tn Heb “the sheaf of the first of your harvest.”

11.anchor: to be accepted for your benefit
11.note: tn Heb “for your acceptance.”

11.anchor: – on the day after the Sabbath the priest is to wave it.
11.note: sn See Lev 7:30 for a note on the “waving” of a “wave offering.”

12.anchor: On the day you wave the sheaf you must also offer
12.note: tn Heb “And you shall make in the day of your waving the sheaf.”

12.anchor: a flawless yearling lamb
12.note: tn Heb “a flawless lamb, a son of its year”; KJV “of the first year”; NLT “a year-old male lamb.”

13.anchor: along with its grain offering, two tenths of an ephah of
13.note: sn See the note on Lev 5:11.

13.anchor: choice wheat flour
13.note: sn See the note on Lev 2:1.

13.anchor: , a soothing aroma,
13.note: sn See the note on Lev 1:9.

13.anchor: and its drink offering, one fourth of a hin of wine.
13.note: tn Heb “wine, one fourth of the hin.” A pre-exilic hin is about 3.6 liters (= ca. 1 quart), so one fourth of a hin would be about one cup.

14.anchor: You must not eat bread, roasted grain, or fresh grain until this very day,
14.note: tn Heb “until the bone of this day.”

14.anchor: until you bring the offering of your God. This is a perpetual statute throughout your generations
14.note: tn Heb “for your generations.”

15.anchor: “‘You must count for yourselves seven weeks from the day after the Sabbath, from the day you bring the wave offering sheaf; they must be complete weeks.
15.note: tn Heb “seven Sabbaths, they shall be complete.” The disjunctive accent under “Sabbaths” precludes the translation “seven complete Sabbaths” (as NASB, NIV; cf. NAB, NRSV, NLT). The text is somewhat awkward, which may explain why the LXX tradition is confused here, either adding “you shall count” again at the end of the verse, or leaving out “they shall be,” or keeping “they shall be” and adding “to you.”
