16.anchor: You must count fifty days – until the day after the seventh Sabbath – and then
16.note: tn Heb “and.” In the translation “then” is supplied to clarify the sequence.

17.anchor: From the places where you live you must bring two loaves of
17.note: tc Smr, LXX, Syriac, Tg. Onq., and Tg. Ps.-J. insert the word חַלּוֹת (khallot, “loaves”; cf. Lev 2:4 and the note there). Even though “loaves” is not explicit in the MT, the number “two” suggests that these are discrete units, not just a measure of flour, so “loaves” should be assumed even in the MT.

17.anchor: bread for a wave offering; they must be made from two tenths of an ephah of fine wheat flour, baked with yeast,
17.note: tn Heb “with leaven.” The noun “leaven” is traditional in English versions (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV), but “yeast” is more commonly used today.

18.anchor: Along with the loaves of bread,
18.note: tn Heb “And you shall present on the bread.”

18.anchor: you must also present seven flawless yearling lambs,
18.note: tn Heb “seven flawless lambs, sons of a year.”

18.anchor: one young bull,
18.note: tn Heb “and one bull, a son of a herd.”

18.anchor: and two rams.
18.note: tc Smr and LXX add “flawless.”

18.anchor: along with their grain offering
18.note: tn Heb “and their grain offering.”
sn See the note on Lev 1:9.

19.anchor: You must also offer
19.note: tn Heb “And you shall make.”

19.anchor: one male goat
19.note: tn Heb “a he-goat of goats.”

20.anchor: and the priest is to wave them – the two lambs
20.note: tn Smr and LXX have the Hebrew article on “lambs.” The syntax of this verse is difficult. The object of the verb (two lambs) is far removed from the verb itself (shall wave) in the MT, and the preposition עַל (’al, “upon”), rendered “along with” in this verse, is also added to the far removed subject (literally, “upon [the] two lambs”; see B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 159). It is clear, however, that the two lambs and the loaves (along with their associated grain and drink offerings) constituted the “wave offering,” which served as the prebend “for the priest.” Burnt and sin offerings (vv. 18-19a) were not included in this (see Lev 7:11-14, 28-36).

21.anchor: “‘On this very day you must proclaim an assembly; it is to be a holy assembly for you.
21.note: tn Heb “And you shall proclaim [an assembly] in the bone of this day; a holy assembly it shall be to you” (see the remarks in B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 160, and the remarks on the LXX rendering in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 367).

21.anchor: You must not do any regular work. This is a perpetual statute in all the places where you live throughout your generations.
21.note: tn Heb “for your generations.”

22.anchor: When you gather in the harvest
22.note: tn Heb “And when you harvest the harvest.”

22.anchor: of your land, you must not completely harvest the corner of your field,
22.note: tn Heb “you shall not complete the corner of your field in your harvest.”

22.anchor: your God.’”
22.note: sn Compare Lev 19:9-10.

24.anchor: “Tell the Israelites, ‘In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you must have a complete rest, a memorial announced by loud horn blasts,
24.note: tn Heb “a memorial of loud blasts.” Although the term for “horn” does not occur here, allowing for the possibility that vocal “shouts” of acclamation are envisioned (see P. J. Budd, Leviticus [NCBC], 325), the “blast” of the shofar (a trumpet made from a ram’s “horn”) is most likely what is intended. On this occasion, the loud blasts on the horn announced the coming of the new year on the first day of the seventh month (see the explanations in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 387, and B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 160).

25.anchor: You must not do any regular work, but
25.note: tn Heb “and.” The Hebrew conjunction ו (vav, “and”) can be considered to have adversative force here (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV).

27.anchor: “The
27.note: tn Heb “Surely the tenth day” or perhaps “Precisely the tenth day.” The Hebrew adverbial particle אַךְ (’akh) is left untranslated by most recent English versions; cf. however NASB “On exactly the tenth day.”

27.anchor: tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement.
27.note: sn See the description of this day and its regulations in Lev 16 and the notes there.

27.anchor: It is to be a holy assembly for you, and you must humble yourselves
27.note: tn Heb “you shall humble your souls.” See the note on Lev 16:29 above.

28.anchor: You must not do any work on this particular day,
28.note: tn Heb “in the bone of this day.”

28.anchor: because it is a day of atonement to make atonement for yourselves
28.note: tn Heb “on you [plural]”; cf. NASB, NRSV “on your behalf.”

29.anchor: Indeed,
29.note: tn The particular כִּי (ki) is taken in an asseverative sense here (“Indeed,” see the NJPS translation).

29.anchor: any person who does not behave with humility on this particular day will be cut off from his people.
29.note: tn Heb “it [i.e., that person; literally “soul,” feminine] shall be cut off from its peoples [plural]”; NLT “from the community.”

30.anchor: As for any person
30.note: tn Heb “And any person.”

30.anchor: who does any work on this particular day, I will exterminate
30.note: tn See HALOT 3 s.v. I אבד hif. Cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT “destroy”; CEV “wipe out.”

30.anchor: that person from the midst of his people!
30.note: tn Heb “its people” (“its” is feminine to agree with “person,” literally “soul,” which is feminine in Hebrew; cf. v. 29).

31.anchor: You must not do any work. This is a perpetual statute throughout your generations

32.anchor: It is a Sabbath of complete rest for you, and you must humble yourselves on the ninth day of the month in the evening, from evening until evening you must observe your Sabbath.”
32.note: tn Heb “you shall rest your Sabbath.”

34.anchor: “Tell the Israelites, ‘On the fifteenth day of this seventh month is the Festival of Temporary Shelters
34.note: tn The rendering “booths” (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV) is probably better than the traditional “tabernacles” in light of the meaning of the term סֻכָּה (sukkah, “hut, booth”), but “booths” are frequently associated with trade shows and craft fairs in contemporary American English. The nature of the celebration during this feast (see the following verses) as a commemoration of the wanderings of the Israelites after they left Egypt suggests that a translation like “temporary shelters” is more appropriate.

35.anchor: On the first day is a holy assembly; you must do no regular work.
35.note: tn Heb “work of service”; KJV “servile work”; NASB “laborious work”; TEV “daily work.”

36.anchor: . It is a solemn assembly day;
36.note: tn The Hebrew term עֲצֶרֶת (’atseret) “solemn assembly [day]” derives from a root associated with restraint or closure. It could refer either to the last day as “closing assembly” day of the festival (e.g., NIV) or a special day of restraint expressed in a “solemn assembly” (e.g., NRSV); cf. NLT “a solemn closing assembly.”

37.anchor: – burnt offering, grain offering, sacrifice, and drink offerings,
37.note: tn The LXX has “[their] burnt offerings, and their sacrifices, and their drink offerings.”

37.anchor: each day according to its regulation,
37.note: tn Heb “a matter of a day in its day”; NAB “as prescribed for each day”; NRSV, NLT “each on its proper day.”

38.note: tn Heb “from to separation.” See BDB 94 s.v. בַּד 1.e for an explanation of this phrase. This phrase is repeated in front of each of the four items in this verse in the Hebrew text, but these have not been translated into English for stylistic reasons. Cf. KJV, NASB “besides”; NRSV “apart from.”

39.note: tn Heb “Surely on the fifteenth day.” The Hebrew adverbial particle אַךְ (’akh) is left untranslated by most recent English versions; however, cf. NASB “On exactly the fifteenth day.”

40.anchor: On the first day you must take for yourselves branches from majestic trees
40.note: tn Heb “fruit of majestic trees,” but the following terms and verses define what is meant by this expression. For extensive remarks on the celebration of this festival in history and tradition see B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 163; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 389-90; and P. J. Budd, Leviticus (NCBC), 328-29.

41.anchor: for seven days in the year. This is a perpetual statute throughout your generations;

42.anchor: You must live in temporary shelters
42.note: tn Heb “in the huts” (again at the end of this verse and in v. 43), perhaps referring to temporary shelters (i.e., huts) made of the foliage referred to in v. 40 (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 389).

44.note: sn E. S. Gerstenberger (Leviticus [OTL], 352) takes v. 44 to be an introduction to another set of festival regulations, perhaps something like those found in Exod 23:14-17. For others this verse reemphasizes the Mosaic authority of the preceding festival regulations (e.g., J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 390).

2.anchor: “When someone has
2.note: tn Heb “A man, if [or when] he has….” The term for “a man, human being” (אָדָם, ’adam; see the note on Lev 1:2) in this case refers to any person among “mankind,” male or female, since either could be afflicted with infections on the skin.

2.anchor: a swelling
2.note: tn Some of the terms for disease or symptoms of disease in this chapter present difficulties for the translator. Most modern English versions render the Hebrew term שְׂאֵת (sÿ’et) as “swelling,” which has been retained here (see the explanation in J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 189). Some have argued that “deeper (עָמֹק, ’amoq) than the skin of his body” in v. 3 means that “this sore was lower than the surrounding skin” (J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:773), in which case “swelling” would be an inappropriate translation of שְׂאֵת in v. 2. Similarly, שְׂאֵת also occurs in v. 19, and then v. 20 raises the issue of whether or not it appears to be “lower (שָׁפָל, shafal) than the skin” (cf. also 14:37 for a mark on the wall of a house), which may mean that the sore sinks below the surface of the skin rather than protruding above it as a swelling would (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 76-77). Thus, one could translate here, for example, “discoloration” (so Milgrom and II שְׂאֵת “spot, blemish on the skin” in HALOT 1301 s.v. II שְׂאֵת) or “local inflammation, boil, mole” (so Levine). However, one could interpret “lower” as “deeper,” i.e., visibly extending below the surface of the skin into the deeper layers as suggested by J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 188, 192. “Swelling” often extends deeply below the surface of the skin, it is certainly a common symptom of skin diseases, and the alternation of these two terms (i.e., “deeper” and “lower”) in vv. 25-26 below shows that they both refer to the same phenomenon (see also the note on v. 20 below), so it is retained in the present translation.

2.anchor: or a scab
2.note: tn The etymology and meaning of this term is unknown. It could mean “scab” (KJV, ASV, NASB) or possibly “rash” (NIV, NLT), “flaking skin,” or an “eruption” (NRSV) of some sort.

2.anchor: or a bright spot
2.note: tn Heb “shiny spot” or “white spot,” but to render this term “white spot” in this chapter would create redundancy in v. 4 where the regular term for “white” occurs alongside this word for “bright spot.”

2.anchor: on the skin of his body
2.note: tn Heb “in the skin of his flesh” as opposed to the head or the beard (v. 29).

2.anchor: that may become a diseased infection,
2.note: tn Heb “a mark [or stroke; or plague] of disease.” In some places in this context (vv. 2, 3) it could be translated “a contagious skin disease.” Although the Hebrew term צָרָעַת (tsara’at) rendered here “diseased” is translated in many English versions as “leprosy,” it does not refer to Hanson’s disease, which is the modern technical understanding of the term “leprosy” (HALOT 1057 s.v. צָרְעַת a). There has been much discussion of the proper meaning of the term and the disease(s) to which it may refer (see, e.g., J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:774-76, 816-26; J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 187-89; and the literature cited by them). The further description of the actual condition in the text suggests that the regulations are concerned with any kind of infectious diseases that are observable on the surface of the skin and, in addition to that, penetrate below the surface of the skin (vv. 3-4) or spread further across the surface of the skin (vv. 5-8). It is true that, in the OT, the term “disease” is often associated specifically with white “scaly” skin diseases that resemble the wasting away of the skin after death (see Milgrom who, in fact, translates “scale disease”; cf., e.g., Exod 4:6-7 and Num 12:9-12, esp. v. 12), but here it appears to be a broader term for any skin disease that penetrates deep or spreads far on the body. Scaly skin diseases would be included in this category, but also other types. Thus, a “swelling,” “scab,” or “bright spot” on the skin might be a symptom of disease, but not necessarily so. In this sense, “diseased” is a technical term. The term “infection” can apply to any “mark” on the skin whether it belongs to the category of “disease” or not (compare and contrast v. 3, where the “infection” is not “diseased,” with v. 4, where the “infection” is found to be “diseased”).

2.anchor: he must be brought to Aaron the priest or one of his sons, the priests.
2.note: tn Or “it shall be reported to Aaron the priest.” This alternative rendering may be better in light of the parallel use of the same expression in Lev 14:2, where the priest had to go outside the camp in order to inspect the person who had been diseased. Since the rendering “he shall be brought to Aaron the priest” might confuse matters there, this expression should be rendered “it shall be reported” both here in 13:2 (cf. also v. 9) and in 14:2. See, however, the further note on 14:2 below, where it is argued that the diseased person would still need to “be brought” to the priest even if this happened outside the camp. Most English versions retain the idea of the afflicted person being “brought” to the priest for inspection.

3.anchor: The priest must then examine the infection
3.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see the infection.”

3.anchor: on the skin of the body, and if the hair
3.note: tn There is no “if” expressed, but the contrast between the priestly finding in this verse and the next verse clearly implies it.

3.anchor: in the infection has turned white and the infection appears to be deeper than the skin of the body,
3.note: tn Heb “and the appearance of the infection is deep ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, “deeper than”) the skin of the his flesh.” See the note on v. 20 below.

3.anchor: then it is a diseased infection,
3.note: tn For the translation “diseased infection” see the note on v. 2 above. Cf. TEV “a dreaded skin disease”; NIV “an infectious skin disease”; NLT “a contagious skin disease.”

3.anchor: so when the priest examines it
3.note: tn The pronoun “it” here refers to the “infection,” not the person who has the infection (cf. the object of “examine” at the beginning of the verse).

3.anchor: he must pronounce the person unclean.
3.note: tn Heb “he shall make him unclean.” The verb is the Piel of טָמֵא (tame’) “to be unclean.” Here it is a so-called “declarative” Piel (i.e., “to declare unclean”), but it also implies that the person is put into the category of actually being “unclean” by the pronouncement itself (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 175; cf. the corresponding opposite in v. 6 below).

4.anchor: “If
4.note: tn Heb “and if.”

4.anchor: it is a white bright spot on the skin of his body, but it does not appear to be deeper than the skin,
4.note: tn Heb “and deep is not its appearance from the skin”; cf. NAB “does not seem to have penetrated below the skin.”

4.anchor: and the hair has not turned white, then the priest is to quarantine the person with the infection for seven days.
4.note: tn Heb “and the priest will shut up the infection seven days.”

5.anchor: The priest must then examine it on the seventh day, and if,
5.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV).

5.anchor: as far as he can see, the infection has stayed the same
5.note: tn Heb “the infection has stood in his eyes”; ASV “if in his eyes the plague be at a stay.”

5.anchor: and has not spread on the skin,
5.note: tn Although there is no expressed “and” at the beginning of this clause, there is in the corresponding clause of v. 6, so it should be assumed here as well.

5.anchor: then the priest is to quarantine the person for another seven days.
5.note: tn Heb “a second seven days.”

6.anchor: The priest must then examine it again on the seventh day,
6.note: tn That is, at the end of the second set of seven days referred to at the end of v. 5, a total of fourteen days after the first appearance before the priest.

6.anchor: and if
6.note: tn Heb “and behold.”

6.anchor: the infection has faded and has not spread on the skin, then the priest is to pronounce the person clean.
6.note: tn Heb “he shall make him clean.” The verb is the Piel of טָהֵר (taher, “to be clean”). Here it is a so-called “declarative” Piel (i.e., “to declare clean”), but it also implies that the person is put into the category of being “clean” by the pronouncement itself (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 176; cf. the corresponding opposite in v. 3 above).

6.anchor: It is a scab,
6.note: tn On the term “scab” see the note on v. 2 above. Cf. NAB “it was merely eczema”; NRSV “only an eruption”; NLT “only a temporary rash.”

6.anchor: so he must wash his clothes
6.note: tn Heb “and he shall wash his clothes.”

7.anchor: If, however, the scab is spreading further
7.note: tn Heb “And if spreading [infinitive absolute] it spreads [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

8.anchor: The priest must then examine it,
8.note: tn The “it” is not expressed but is to be understood. It refers to the “infection” (cf. the note on v. 2 above).

8.anchor: and if
8.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV).

8.anchor: the scab has spread on the skin, then the priest is to pronounce the person unclean.
8.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’, cf. the note on v. 3 above).

9.anchor: “When someone has a diseased infection,
9.note: tn Heb “When there is an infection of disease in a man.” The term for “a man; a human being” (אָדָם, ’adam; see the note on Lev 1:2 and cf. v. 2 above) refers to any person among “mankind,” male or female. For the rendering “diseased infection” see the note on v. 2 above.

10.anchor: The priest will then examine it,
10.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see.” The pronoun “it” is unexpressed, but it should be assumed and it refers to the infection (cf. the note on v. 8 above).

10.anchor: and if
10.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV).

10.anchor: a white swelling is on the skin, it has turned the hair white, and there is raw flesh in the swelling,
10.note: tn Heb “and rawness [i.e., something living] of living flesh is in the swelling”; KJV, NASB, NRSV “quick raw flesh.”

11.anchor: it is a chronic
11.note: tn The term rendered here “chronic” is a Niphal participle meaning “grown old” (HALOT 448 s.v. II ישׁן nif.2). The idea is that this is an old enduring skin disease that keeps on developing or recurring.

11.anchor: disease on the skin of his body,
11.note: tn Heb “in the skin of his flesh” as opposed to the head or the beard (v. 29; cf. v. 2 above).

11.anchor: so the priest is to pronounce him unclean.
11.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’, cf. the note on v. 3 above).

11.anchor: The priest
11.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the priest) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: must not merely quarantine him, for he is unclean.
11.note: sn Instead of just the normal quarantine isolation, this condition calls for the more drastic and enduring response stated in Lev 13:45-46. Raw flesh, of course, sometimes oozes blood to one degree or another, and blood flows are by nature impure (see, e.g., Lev 12 and 15; cf. J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 191).

12.anchor: If, however, the disease breaks out
12.note: tn Heb “And if spreading [infinitive absolute] it spreads out [finite verb].” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

12.anchor: on the skin so that the disease covers all the skin of the person with the infection
12.note: tn Heb “all the skin of the infection,” but see v. 4 above.

12.anchor: from his head to his feet, as far as the priest can see,
12.note: tn Heb “to all the appearance of the eyes of the priest.”

13.anchor: the priest must then examine it,
13.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see.” The pronoun “it” is unexpressed, but it should be assumed and it refers to the infection (cf. the note on v. 8 above).

13.anchor: and if
13.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV, NASB).

13.anchor: the disease covers his whole body, he is to pronounce the person with the infection clean.
13.note: tn Heb “he shall pronounce the infection clean,” but see v. 4 above. Also, this is another use of the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher; cf. the note on v. 6 above).

13.anchor: He has turned all white, so he is clean.
13.note: tn Heb “all of him has turned white, and he is clean.”

14.anchor: But whenever raw flesh appears in it
14.note: tn Heb “and in the day of there appears in it living flesh.” Some English versions render this as “open sores” (cf. NCV, TEV, NLT).

15.anchor: so the priest is to examine the raw flesh
15.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see the living flesh.”

15.anchor: and pronounce him unclean
15.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’; cf. the note on v. 3 above).

16.anchor: If, however,
16.note: tn Heb “Or if/when.”

16.anchor: the raw flesh once again turns white,
16.note: tn Heb “the living flesh returns and is turned/changed to white.” The Hebrew verb “returns” is שׁוּב (shuv), which often functions adverbially when combined with a second verb as it is here (cf. “and is turned”) and, in such cases, is usually rendered “again” (see, e.g., GKC 386-87 §120.g). Another suggestion is that here שׁוּב means “to recede” (cf., e.g., 2 Kgs 20:9), so one could translate “the raw flesh recedes and turns white.” This would mean that the new “white” skin “has grown over” the raw flesh (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 79).

17.anchor: The priest will then examine it,
17.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see it.”

17.anchor: and if
17.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV, NASB).

17.anchor: the infection has turned white, the priest is to pronounce the person with the infection clean
17.note: tn Heb “the priest shall pronounce the infection clean,” but see v. 4 above. Also, this is another use of the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher, cf. the note on v. 6 above).

18.anchor: “When someone’s body has a boil on its skin
18.note: tc Heb (MT) reads, “And flesh if/when there is in it, in its skin, a boil.” Smr has only “in it,” not “in its skin,” and a few medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate have only “in its skin” (cf. v. 24 below), not “in it.” It does not effect the meaning of the verse, but one is tempted to suggest that “in it” (בוֹ, vo) was added in error as a partial dittography from the beginning of “in its skin” (בְעֹרוֹ, vÿ’oro).

19.anchor: and in the place of the boil there is a white swelling or a reddish white bright spot, he must show himself to the priest.
19.note: tn Some English versions translate “it shall be shown to [or “be seen by”] the priest,” taking the infection to be the subject of the verb (e.g., KJV, NASB, RSV, NRSV). Based on the Hebrew grammar there is no way to be sure which is intended.

20.anchor: The priest will then examine it,
20.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see.” The pronoun “it” is unexpressed, but it should be assumed and it refers to the infection (cf. the note on v. 8 above).

20.anchor: and if
20.note: tn Heb “and behold.”

20.anchor: it appears to be deeper than the skin
20.note: tn Heb “and behold its appearance is low (שָׁפָל, shafal) ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, “lower than”) the skin.” Compare “deeper” in v. 3 above where, however, a different word is used (עָמֹק, ’amoq), and see the note on “swelling” in v. 1 above (cf. J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 192; note that, contrary to the MT, Tg. Onq. has עָמֹק in this verse as well as v. 4). The alternation of these two terms (i.e., “deeper” and “lower”) in vv. 25-26 below shows that they both refer to the same phenomenon. Some have argued that “this sore was lower than the surrounding skin” (J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:773, 788), in which case “swelling” would be an inappropriate translation of שְׂאֵת (sÿ’et) in v. 19. It seems unlikely, however, that the surface of a “boil” would sink below the surface of the surrounding skin. The infectious pus etc. that makes up a boil normally causes swelling.

20.anchor: and its hair has turned white, then the priest is to pronounce the person unclean.
20.note: tn The declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’, cf. the note on v. 3 above).

20.anchor: It is a diseased infection that has broken out in the boil.
20.note: tn Heb “It is an infection of disease. In the boil it has broken out.” For the rendering “diseased infection” see the note on v. 2 above.

21.anchor: If, however,
21.note: tn Heb “and if.”

21.anchor: the priest examines it, and
21.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV, NASB).

21.anchor: there is no white hair in it, it is not deeper than the skin, and it has faded, then the priest is to quarantine him for seven days.
21.note: tn Heb “and the priest will shut him up seven days.”

22.anchor: If
22.note: tn Heb “and if.”

22.anchor: it is spreading further
22.note: tn Heb “is indeed spreading.”

22.anchor: on the skin, then the priest is to pronounce him unclean.
22.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’, cf. the note on v. 3 above).

23.anchor: But if the bright spot stays in its place and has not spread,
23.note: tn Heb “and if under it the bright spot stands, it has not spread.”

23.anchor: it is the scar of the boil, so the priest is to pronounce him clean.
23.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher, cf. the note on v. 6 above).

24.anchor: “When a body has a burn on its skin
24.note: tn Heb “Or a body, if there is in its skin a burn of fire.”

25.anchor: the priest must examine it,
25.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see it.”

25.anchor: and if
25.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV).

25.anchor: the hair has turned white in the bright spot and it appears to be deeper than the skin,
25.note: tn Heb “and its appearance is deep ‘from’ [comparative מִן (min) meaning ‘deeper than’] the skin.”

25.anchor: it is a disease that has broken out in the burn.
25.note: tn Heb “it is a disease. In the burn it has broken out.”

25.anchor: The priest is to pronounce the person unclean.
25.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’; cf. the note on v. 3 above).

25.anchor: It is a diseased infection.
25.note: tn For the rendering “diseased infection” see the note on v. 2 above.

26.anchor: If, however,
26.note: tn Heb “and if.”

26.anchor: the priest examines it and
26.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV); NASB “and indeed.”

26.anchor: there is no white hair in the bright spot, it is not deeper than the skin,
26.note: tn Heb “and low it is not ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, “lower than”) the skin.” See the note on v. 20 above. Cf. TEV “not deeper than the surrounding skin.”

26.anchor: and it has faded, then the priest is to quarantine him for seven days.
26.note: tn Heb “and the priest will shut him up seven days.”

27.anchor: The priest must then examine it on the seventh day, and if it is spreading further
27.note: tn Heb “is indeed spreading.”

27.anchor: on the skin, then the priest is to pronounce him unclean. It is a diseased infection.
27.note: tn For the rendering “diseased infection” see the note on v. 2 above.

28.anchor: But if the bright spot stays in its place, has not spread on the skin,
28.note: tn Heb “and if under it the bright spot stands, it has not spread in the skin.”

28.anchor: and it has faded, then it is the swelling of the burn, so the priest is to pronounce him clean,
28.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher; cf. the note on v. 6 above).

29.anchor: “When a man or a woman has an infection on the head or in the beard,
29.note: tn Heb “And a man or a woman if there is in him an infection in head or in beard.” sn The shift here is from diseases that are on the (relatively) bare skin of the body to the scalp area of the male or female head or the bearded area of the male face.

30.anchor: the priest is to examine the infection,
30.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see the infection.”

30.anchor: and if
30.note: tn Heb “and behold.”

30.anchor: it appears to be deeper than the skin
30.note: tn Heb “its appearance is deep ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, “deeper than”) the skin.”

30.anchor: and the hair in it is reddish yellow and thin, then the priest is to pronounce the person unclean.
30.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָמֵא (tame’; cf. the note on v. 3 above).

30.anchor: It is scall,
30.note: tn The exact identification of this disease is unknown. Cf. KJV “dry scall”; NASB “a scale”; NIV, NCV, NRSV “an itch”; NLT “a contagious skin disease.” For a discussion of “scall” disease in the hair, which is a crusty scabby disease of the skin under the hair that also affects the hair itself, see J. E. Hartley, Leviticus (WBC), 192-93, and J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:793-94. The Hebrew word rendered “scall” (נֶתֶק, neteq) is related to a verb meaning “to tear; to tear out; to tear apart.” It may derive from the scratching and/or the tearing out of the hair or the scales of the skin in response to the itching sensation caused by the disease.

30.anchor: a disease of the head or the beard.
30.note: tn Heb “It is scall. It is the disease of the head or the beard.”

31.anchor: But if the priest examines the scall infection and it does not appear to be deeper than the skin,
31.note: tn Heb “and behold there is not its appearance deep ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, meaning “deeper than”) the skin.”

31.anchor: and there is no black hair in it, then the priest is to quarantine the person with the scall infection for seven days.
31.note: tn Heb “and the priest will shut up the infection of the scall seven days.”

32.anchor: The priest must then examine the infection on the seventh day, and if
the scall has not spread, there is no reddish yellow hair in it, and the scall does not appear to be deeper than the skin,
32.note: tn Heb “and the appearance of the scall is not deep ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, meaning “deeper than”) the skin.”

33.anchor: then the individual is to shave himself,
33.note: tn The shaving is done by the one who has the infection. Although KJV, ASV have the passive “he shall be shaven” here, most modern English versions have the reflexive “shall shave himself” (so NAB).

33.anchor: but he must not shave the area affected by the scall,
33.note: tn Heb “but the scall shall he not shave” (so KJV, ASV); NIV “except for the diseased area.”

33.anchor: and the priest is to quarantine the person with the scall for another seven days.
33.note: tn Heb “and the priest will shut up the scall a second seven days.”

34.anchor: The priest must then examine the scall on the seventh day, and if
34.note: tn Heb “and behold” (so KJV, ASV).

34.anchor: the scall has not spread on the skin and it does not appear to be deeper than the skin,
34.note: tn Heb “and its appearance is not deep ‘from’ (comparative מִן, min, meaning “deeper than”) the skin.”

34.anchor: then the priest is to pronounce him clean.
34.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher, cf. the note on v. 6 above).

35.anchor: If, however, the scall spreads further
35.note: tn Heb “And if spreading (infinitive absolute) it spreads further (finite verb).” For the infinitive absolute used to highlight contrast rather than emphasis see GKC 343 §113.p.

36.anchor: then the priest is to examine it, and if
the scall has spread on the skin the priest is not to search further for reddish yellow hair.
36.note: tn Heb “the priest shall not search to the reddish yellow hair.”
tn Heb “he”; the referent (the affected person) is specified in the translation for clarity (likewise in the following verse).

37.anchor: If, as far as the priest can see, the scall has stayed the same
37.note: tn Heb “and if in his eyes the infection has stood.”

37.anchor: and black hair has sprouted in it, the scall has been healed; the person is clean. So the priest is to pronounce him clean.
37.note: tn This is the declarative Piel of the verb טָהֵר (taher, cf. the note on v. 6 above).

39.anchor: the priest is to examine them,
39.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see.”

39.anchor: the bright spots on the skin of their body are faded white, it is a harmless rash that has broken out on the skin. The person is clean.
39.note: tn Heb “he,” but the regulation applies to a man or a woman (v. 38a). In the translation “the person” is used to specify the referent more clearly.

40.anchor: “When a man’s head is bare so that he is balding in back,
40.note: tn Heb “And a man, when his head is rubbed bare, he is bald-headed.” The translation offered here, referring to the back of the head (i.e., the area from the top of the head sloping backwards), is based on the contrast between this condition and that of the following verse. See also B. A. Levine, Leviticus (JPSTC), 82.

41.anchor: If his head is bare on the forehead
41.note: tn Heb “And if from the front edge of his face, his head is rubbed bare.” See the note on v. 40 above.

41.anchor: so that he is balding in front,
41.note: tn The rendering “balding in front” corresponds to the location of the bareness at the beginning of the verse.

43.anchor: The priest is to examine it,
43.note: tn Heb “and the priest shall see it” (cf. KJV). The MT has “him/it” which some take to refer to the person as a whole (i.e., “him”; see, e.g., J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:770; NIV, NRSV, etc.), while others take it as a reference to the “infection” (נֶגַע, nega’) in v. 42 (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 172, 177). Smr has “her/it,” which would probably refer to “disease” (צָרַעַת, tsara’at) in v. 42. The general pattern in the chapter suggests that “it,” either the infection or the disease, is the object of the examination (see, e.g., v. 3 above and v. 50 below).

43.anchor: the swelling of the infection is reddish white in the back or front bald area like the appearance of a disease on the skin of the body,
43.note: tn Heb “like appearance of disease of skin of flesh.”

44.anchor: he is a diseased man. He is unclean. The priest must surely pronounce him unclean because of his infection on his head.
44.note: tn Or perhaps translate, “His infection [is] on his head,” as a separate independent sentence (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV). There is no causal expression in the Hebrew text connecting these two clauses, but the logical relationship between them seems to be causal.

45.anchor: “As for the diseased person who has the infection,
45.note: tn Heb “And the diseased one who in him is the infection.”

45.anchor: his clothes must be torn, the hair of his head must be unbound, he must cover his mustache,
45.note: tn Heb “and his head shall be unbound, and he shall cover on [his] mustache.” Tearing one’s clothing, allowing the hair to hang loose rather than bound up in a turban, and covering the mustache on the upper lip are all ways of expressing shame, grief, or distress (cf., e.g., Lev 10:6 and Micah 3:7).

46.anchor: The whole time he has the infection
46.note: tn Heb “All the days which the infection is in him.”

47.anchor: “When a garment has a diseased infection in it,
47.note: tn Heb “And the garment, if there is in it a mark of disease.”

47.anchor: whether a wool or linen garment,
47.note: tn Heb “in a wool garment or in a linen garment.”

48.anchor: or in the warp or woof
48.note: sn The warp (vertical) and woof (horizontal) thread may be two different sets of thread not yet woven together, or they may refer to two different kinds of thread already woven, in which case one might have the disease in it while the other does not. See the explanation in J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:809-10.

48.anchor: of the linen or the wool, or in leather or anything made of leather,
48.note: tn Heb “in any handiwork of skin” (cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV); most other modern English versions have “leather.”

49.note: tn Heb “and the infection is.” This clause is conditional in force, and is translated as such by almost all English versions.

50.anchor: The priest is to examine and then quarantine the article with the infection for seven days.
50.note: tn Heb “And the priest shall see the infection and he shall shut up the infection seven days.”

51.anchor: He must then examine the infection on the seventh day. If the infection has spread in the garment, or in the warp, or in the woof, or in the leather – whatever the article into which the leather was made
51.note: tn Heb “to all which the leather was made into a handiwork.”

53.anchor: But if the priest examines it and
53.note: tn Heb “And if the priest sees and behold”; NASB “and indeed.”

54.anchor: the priest is to command that they wash whatever has the infection and quarantine it for another seven days.

55.anchor: The priest must then examine it after the infection has been washed out, and if
the infection has not changed its appearance
55.note: tn Heb “the infection has not changed its eye.” Smr has “its/his eyes,” as in vv. 5 and 37, but here it refers to the appearance of the article of cloth or leather, unlike vv. 5 and 37 where there is a preposition attached and it refers to the eyes of the priest.

55.anchor: even though the infection has not spread, it is unclean. You must burn it up in the fire. It is a fungus, whether on the back side or front side of the article.
55.note: tn The terms “back side” and “front side” are the same as those used in v. 42 for the “back or front bald area” of a man’s head. The exact meaning of these terms when applied to articles of cloth or leather is uncertain. It could refer, for example, to the inside versus the outside of a garment, or the back versus the front side of an article of cloth or leather. See J. Milgrom, Leviticus (AB), 1:814, for various possibilities.

56.anchor: But if the priest has examined it and
56.note: tn Heb “And if the priest saw and behold….”

56.anchor: the infection has faded after it has been washed, he is to tear it out of
56.note: tn Heb “and he shall tear it from.”

57.note: tn Heb “And if”; NIV, NCV “But if”; NAB “If, however.”

58.anchor: But the garment or the warp or the woof or any article of leather which you wash and infection disappears from it
58.note: tn Heb “and the infection turns aside from them.”

59.note: sn The Hebrew term translated “law” (תוֹרָה, torah) introduces here a summary or colophon for all of Lev 13. Similar summaries are found in Lev 7:37-38; 11:46-47; 14:54-57; and 15:32-33.

59.anchor: of the diseased infection in the garment of wool or linen, or the warp or woof, or any article of leather, for pronouncing it clean or unclean.
59.note: tn These are declarative Piel forms of the verbs טָהֵר (taher) and טָמֵא (tame’) respectively (cf. the notes on vv. 3 and 6 above).

2.anchor: “Tell the Israelites, ‘When a woman produces offspring
2.note: tn Heb “produces seed” (Hiphil of זָרַע, zara’; used only elsewhere in Gen 1:11-12 for plants “producing” their own “seed”), referring to the process of childbearing as a whole, from conception to the time of birth (H. D. Preuss, TDOT 4:144; cf. J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 164-65; and J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:742-43). Smr and LXX have Niphal “be impregnated” (see, e.g., Num 5:28); note KJV “If a woman have conceived seed” (cf. ASV, NAB, NRSV; also NIV, NLT “becomes pregnant”).

2.anchor: and bears a male child,
2.note: sn The regulations for the “male child” in vv. 2-4 contrast with those for the “female child” in v. 5 (see the note there).

2.anchor: she will be unclean seven days, as she is unclean during the days of her menstruation.
2.note: tn Heb “as the days of the menstrual flow [nom.] of her menstruating [q. inf.] she shall be unclean” (R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 1:925-26; the verb appears only in this verse in the OT). Cf. NASB “as in the days of her menstruation”; NLT “during her menstrual period”; NIV “during her monthly period.”sn See Lev 15:19-24 for the standard purity regulations for a woman’s menstrual period.

3.anchor: On
3.note: tn Heb “and in….”

3.anchor: the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin
3.note: tn This rendering, “the flesh of his foreskin,” is literal. Based on Lev 15:2-3, one could argue that the Hebrew word for “flesh” here (בָּשָׂר, basar) is euphemistic for the male genitals and therefore translate “the foreskin of his member” (see, e.g., J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:748). A number of English versions omit this reference to the foreskin and mention only circumcision, presumably for euphemistic reasons (cf. NIV, NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).

4.anchor: Then she will remain
4.note: tn Heb “sit, dwell” (יָשָׁב, yashav) normally means “to sit, to dwell”), but here it means “to remain, to stay” in the same condition for a period of time (cf., e.g., Gen 24:55).

4.anchor: thirty-three days in blood purity.
4.note: tn Heb “in bloods of purification” or “purifying” or “purity”; NASB “in the blood of her purification”; NRSV “her time of blood purification.” See the following note.

4.anchor: She must not touch anything holy and she must not enter the sanctuary until the days of her purification are fulfilled.
4.note: tn The initial seven days after the birth of a son were days of blood impurity for the woman as if she were having her menstrual period. Her impurity was contagious during this period, so no one should touch her or even furniture on which she has sat or reclined (Lev 15:19-23), lest they too become impure. Even her husband would become impure for seven days if he had sexual intercourse with her during this time (Lev 15:24; cf. 18:19). The next thirty-three days were either “days of purification, purifying” or “days of purity,” depending on how one understands the abstract noun טֹהֳרָה (toharah, “purification, purity”) in this context. During this time the woman could not touch anything holy or enter the sanctuary, but she was no longer contagious like she had been during the first seven days. She could engage in normal everyday life, including sexual intercourse, without fear of contaminating anyone else (B. A. Levine, Leviticus [JPSTC], 73-74; cf. J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:749-50). Thus, in a sense, the thirty-three days were a time of blood “purity” (cf. the present translation) as compared to the previous seven days of blood “impurity,” but they were also a time of blood “purification” (or “purifying”) as compared to the time after the thirty-three days, when the blood atonement had been made and she was pronounced “clean” by the priest (see vv. 6-8 below). In other words, the thirty-three day period was a time of “blood” (flow), but this was “pure blood,” as opposed to the blood of the first seven days.

5.anchor: If she bears a female child, she will be impure fourteen days as during her menstrual flow, and she will remain sixty-six days in
5.note: tn Heb “on purity blood.” The preposition here is עַל (’al) rather than בְּ (bÿ, as it is in the middle of v. 4), but no doubt the same meaning is intended.

5.anchor: blood purity.
5.note: tn For clarification of the translation here, see the notes on vv. 2-4 above. sn The doubling of the time after the birth of a female child is puzzling (see the remarks in J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:750-51; and G. J. Wenham, Leviticus [NICOT], 188). Some have argued, for example, that it derives from the relative status of the sexes, or a supposed longer blood flow for the birth of a woman, or even to compensate for the future menstrual periods of the female just born. Perhaps there is a better explanation. First, a male child must be circumcised on the eighth day, so the impurity of the mother could not last beyond the first seven days lest it interfere with the circumcision rite. A female child, of course, was not circumcised, so the impurity of the mother would not interfere and the length of the impure time could be extended further. Second, it would be natural to expect that the increased severity of the blood flow after childbirth, as compared to that of a woman’s menstrual period, would call for a longer period of impurity than the normal seven days of the menstrual period impurity (compare Lev 15:19 with 15:25-30). Third, this suggests that the fourteen day impurity period for the female child would have been more appropriate, and the impurity period for the birth of a male child had to be shortened. Fourth, not only the principle of multiples of seven but also multiples of forty applies to this reckoning. Since the woman’s blood discharge after bearing a child continues for more than seven days, her discharge keeps her from contact with sacred things for a longer period of time in order to avoid contaminating the tabernacle (note Lev 15:31). This ended up totaling forty days for the birth of a male child (seven plus thirty-three) and a corresponding doubling of the second set of days for the woman (fourteen plus sixty-six). See R. E. Averbeck, NIDOTTE 2:368-70. The fact that the offerings were the same for either a male or a female infant (vv. 6-8) suggests that the other differences in the regulations are not due to the notion that a male child had greater intrinsic value than a female child (J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 169).

6.anchor: “‘When
6.note: tn Heb “And when” (so KJV, NASB). Many recent English versions leave the conjunction untranslated.

6.anchor: the days of her purification are completed for a son or for a daughter, she must bring a one year old lamb
6.note: tn Heb “a lamb the son of his year”; KJV “a lamb of the first year” (NRSV “in its first year”); NAB “a yearling lamb.”

6.anchor: for a burnt offering
6.note: sn See the note on Lev 1:3 regarding the “burnt offering.”

6.anchor: and a young pigeon or turtledove for a sin offering
6.note: sn See the note on Lev 4:3 regarding the term “sin offering.”

7.anchor: The priest
7.note: tn Heb “and he” (i.e., the priest mentioned at the end of v. 6). The referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.

7.anchor: and make atonement
7.note: sn See the note on Lev 1:4 “make atonement.” The purpose of sin offering “atonement,” in particular, was to purge impurities from the tabernacle (see Lev 15:31 and 16:5-19, 29-34), whether they were caused by physical uncleannesses or by sins and iniquities. In this case, the woman has not “sinned” morally by having a child. Even Mary brought such offerings for giving birth to Jesus (Luke 2:22-24), though she certainly did not “sin” in giving birth to him. Note that the result of bringing this “sin offering” was “she will be clean,” not “she will be forgiven” (cf. Lev 4:20, 26, 31, 35; 5:10, 13). The impurity of the blood flow has caused the need for this “sin offering,” not some moral or relational infringement of the law (contrast Lev 4:2, “When a person sins by straying unintentionally from any of the commandments of the Lord”).

7.anchor: on her behalf, and she will be clean
7.note: tn Or “she will be[come] pure.”

7.anchor: from her flow of blood.
7.note: tn Heb “from her source [i.e., spring] of blood,” possibly referring to the female genital area, not just the “flow of blood” itself (as suggested by J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:761). Cf. ASV “from the fountain of her blood.”

8.anchor: If she cannot afford a sheep,
8.note: tn Heb “If her hand cannot find the sufficiency of a sheep.” Many English versions render this as “lamb.”

8.anchor: then she must take two turtledoves or two young pigeons,
8.note: tn Heb “from the sons of the pigeon,” referring either to “young pigeons” or “various species of pigeon” (contrast J. Milgrom, Leviticus [AB], 1:168, with J. E. Hartley, Leviticus [WBC], 14; cf. Lev 1:14 and esp. 5:7-10).

8.anchor: one for a burnt offering and one for a sin offering, and the priest is to make atonement on her behalf, and she will be clean.’”
8.note: tn Or “she will be[come] pure.”
